1	\chapter{The Quot scheme}
2	\label{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the Quot-foundations layer of the
6	% Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman Quot-scheme construction
7	% \(\mathrm{Quot}^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\): the per-fiber Hilbert polynomial, the
8	% Quot functor of \(T\)-flat coherent quotients of \(E_T\) on
9	% \(X_T = X \times_S T\), and the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} together with
10	% its representability. The Quot functor underlies the relative Picard
11	% functor via the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
12	% -- the case \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\) of the Quot functor.
13	%
14	% LOAD-BEARING SUB-PREREQUISITE. The Quot construction embeds Quot as a
15	% locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian
16	% \(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\).
17	% Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} -- only
18	% a linear-algebra Grassmannian as a finite-rank-quotient variety. The
19	% scheme-level Grassmannian over an arbitrary noetherian base is therefore an
20	% in-project sub-build. This chapter introduces it as
21	% \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} and states its representability
22	% (\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
23	
24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
43	
44	% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1;
45	% FGA Explained Ch.~5, AMS MSM 123 (read from
46	% references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L287-L500).
47	% The Hartshorne reference for the polynomial-eventually
48	% property of Euler characteristics is [Hartshorne], III.5.2 (the scanned
49	% PDF at references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf has no text layer, so
50	% the verbatim source-quote is taken from
51	% Nitsure \S 1; Hartshorne III.5.2 is cited in prose as the textbook
52	% reference).
53	
54	\section{Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf}
55	\label{sec:hilbert_polynomial}
56	
57	\begin{definition}\leanok
58	[Hilbert polynomial]
59	  \label{def:hilbert_polynomial}
60	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial}
61	  \uses{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
62	  % ENCODING. `def:hilbert_polynomial` is formalized via the GRADED HILBERT
63	  % FUNCTION of the section module ⊕_{m≥0} Γ(X_s, F_s ⊗ L_s^{⊗m})
64	  % (def:sectionGradedModule), whose polynomiality is extracted in
65	  % thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule from the graded Hilbert–Serre rationality
66	  % lemma (lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational) together with the Mathlib lemma
67	  % Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly (lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib). The
68	  % construction uses only H^0; for m≫0 it agrees with the Euler characteristic
69	  % χ by Serre vanishing, so it is the classical Hilbert polynomial and no
70	  % numerical invariant is lost.
71	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
72	  % Polynomials" (read from
73	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L478).
74	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
75	  % "Let \(X\) be a finite type scheme over
76	  %  a field \(k\), together with a line bundle \(L\).
77	  %  Recall that if \(F\) is a coherent sheaf on
78	  %  \(X\) whose support is proper over \(k\), then
79	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\) of \(F\) is defined by
80	  %  the function
81	  %  \(\){\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
82	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})\(\)
83	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
84	  %  coherence and properness conditions. The fact that \(\chi(F(m))\)
85	  %  is indeed a polynomial in \(m\) under the above assumption is a special
86	  %  case of what is known as Snapper's Lemma (see Kleiman [K] for a proof).
87	  %
88	  %  Let \(X\to S\) be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
89	  %  and let \(L\) be a line bundle on \(X\). Let \(\F\)
90	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
91	  %  \(X\) whose schematic support is proper over \(S\). Then for each \(s\in S\),
92	  %  we get a polynomial \({\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]\)
93	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
94	  %  the restriction \(\F_s = F|_{X_s}\) of \(\F\) to the fiber \(X_s\) over \(s\),
95	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\).
96	  %  If \(\F\) is flat over \(S\) then the function \(s\mapsto {\Phi}_s\)
97	  %  from the set of points of \(S\) to the polynomial ring \(\Q[\lambda]\) is
98	  %  known to be locally constant on \(S\)."
99	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
100	  III.5.2.}
101	  Let \(X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism of noetherian schemes and let \(L\)
102	  be a line bundle on \(X\). For a coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) whose
103	  schematic support is proper over \(S\) and a point \(s \in S\), write \(X_s\)
104	  for the fibre over \(s\), \(\mathcal{F}_s = \mathcal{F}|_{X_s}\) and
105	  \(L_s = L|_{X_s}\). The \emph{Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}\) at \(s\)
106	  relative to \(L\)} is the unique polynomial
107	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) that agrees, for all
108	  \(m \gg 0\), with the graded Hilbert function
109	  \[
110	    m \;\longmapsto\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr)
111	  \]
112	  of the section module
113	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
114	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), viewed as a finitely generated graded module
115	  over the homogeneous section ring
116	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
117	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}). Existence and uniqueness of
118	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) are the content of
119	  \cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}.
120	
121	  This is the classical Hilbert polynomial. When \(\mathcal{F}_s\) is coherent
122	  and \(L_s\) is ample on the proper \(\kappa(s)\)-scheme \(X_s\), Serre vanishing
123	  gives \(H^i(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}) = 0\) for all \(i > 0\)
124	  and \(m \gg 0\), so for \(m \gg 0\)
125	  \[
126	    \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
127	    \;=\; \chi(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
128	    \;=\; \sum_{i \ge 0} (-1)^i \dim_{\kappa(s)} H^i(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}),
129	  \]
130	  and hence \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) coincides with the Euler-characteristic
131	  Hilbert polynomial of Snapper's Lemma -- no numerical invariant is lost. The
132	  construction above, however, uses only the global sections \(H^0\): the higher
133	  cohomology enters solely through the classical statement that the two functions
134	  agree for \(m \gg 0\), never through the definition of \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\)
135	  itself. When \(\mathcal{F}\) is \(S\)-flat the function
136	  \(s \mapsto \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is locally constant on \(S\), so \(\Phi\) is
137	  well-defined on connected components.
138	\end{definition}
139	
140	\section{Graded Hilbert polynomial}
141	\label{sec:graded_hilbert_polynomial}
142	
143	This section gives the graded-Hilbert-function construction that underlies
144	\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Working over a single fibre \(X_s\) with its
145	ample line bundle \(L_s\) and coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\), it builds the
146	section graded ring (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and module
147	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), records their finite generation
148	(\cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}), and extracts the Hilbert polynomial
149	(\cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}) by combining the graded
150	Hilbert--Serre rationality of the Hilbert series
151	(\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) with Mathlib's polynomial-extraction
152	lemma (\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}). The route uses only \(H^0\)
153	(global sections), so it is independent of higher coherent cohomology.
154	
155	\begin{definition}
156	[Section graded ring]
157	  \label{def:sectionGradedRing}
158	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing}
159	  % NOTE: Formalization is blocked on the absence of tensor products for sheaves of modules in
160	  % Mathlib at the pinned commit. The degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\)
161	  % requires a tensor power \(L_s^{\otimes m}\) of a line bundle; this infrastructure (tensor
162	  % product of sheaves of modules with lax-monoidal global sections) is not yet available, making
163	  % this definition a prerequisite sub-build.
164	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
165	  % sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert Polynomials" (read from
166	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
167	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
168	  % "Let $X$ be a finite type scheme over
169	  %  a field $k$, together with a line bundle $L$.
170	  %  Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
171	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
172	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
173	  %  the function
174	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
175	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
176	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
177	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
178	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); the section graded
179	  ring is the homogeneous coordinate ring of [Hartshorne], I.7.}
180	  Let \(s \in S\) and let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on the fibre \(X_s\), a scheme
181	  of finite type over the residue field \(\kappa(s)\). The \emph{section graded
182	  ring} of \(L_s\) is the graded commutative ring
183	  \[
184	    R(X_s, L_s) \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
185	  \]
186	  with degree-\(m\) component \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) and multiplication
187	  the tensor product of sections
188	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
189	  \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'}) \to \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\),
190	  \(\sigma \otimes \tau \mapsto \sigma \cdot \tau\). Its degree-zero component is
191	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s})\), which contains \(\kappa(s)\). When \(X_s\) is
192	  proper over \(\kappa(s)\) and \(L_s\) is ample, \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely
193	  generated -- hence Noetherian -- graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra (a suitable
194	  Veronese power of \(L_s\) is very ample and embeds \(X_s\) into a projective
195	  space, exhibiting \(R(X_s, L_s)\) up to a finite extension as a quotient of a
196	  polynomial ring).
197	\end{definition}
198	
199	\begin{definition}
200	[Section graded module]
201	  \label{def:sectionGradedModule}
202	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule}
203	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing}
204	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
205	  % Polynomials" (read from
206	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
207	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
208	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
209	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
210	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
211	  %  the function
212	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
213	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
214	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
215	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
216	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); cf.\ [Hartshorne],
217	  I.7.}
218	  Let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on the fibre \(X_s\) and \(L_s\) a
219	  line bundle on \(X_s\). The \emph{section graded module} of \(\mathcal{F}_s\)
220	  twisted by \(L_s\) is the graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module
221	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing})
222	  \[
223	    M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)
224	    \;=\; \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr),
225	  \]
226	  with degree-\(m\) component the \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space
227	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) and scalar action given
228	  by the section tensor product
229	  \(\Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\kappa(s)}
230	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})
231	  \to \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes (m+m')})\). Its graded Hilbert
232	  function is \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)}
233	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\).
234	\end{definition}
235	
236	\begin{lemma}
237	[Finite generation of the section graded module]
238	  \label{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg}
239	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_fg}
240	  \uses{def:sectionGradedModule, def:sectionGradedRing}
241	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
242	  % Polynomials" (read from
243	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L453-L464).
244	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
245	  % "Recall that if $F$ is a coherent sheaf on
246	  %  $X$ whose support is proper over $k$, then
247	  %  the \textbf{Hilbert polynomial} ${\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]$ of $F$ is defined by
248	  %  the function
249	  %  $${\Phi}(m) = \chi(F(m)) =
250	  %  \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \dim_k H^i(X, F\otimes L^{\otimes m})$$
251	  %  where the dimensions of the cohomologies are finite because of the
252	  %  coherence and properness conditions."
253	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5); Serre's theorem on
254	  ample line bundles, [Hartshorne], I.7 / II.5.}
255	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
256	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
257	  Then the section graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) is a
258	  Noetherian graded ring, and the section graded module
259	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}) is a finitely
260	  generated graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module. In particular each component
261	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a finite-dimensional
262	  \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space.
263	\end{lemma}
264	
265	\begin{proof}
266	  \uses{def:sectionGradedRing, def:sectionGradedModule}
267	  Since \(L_s\) is ample and \(X_s\) is proper over \(\kappa(s)\), a Veronese
268	  power \(L_s^{\otimes e}\) is very ample and gives a closed immersion
269	  \(X_s \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^N_{\kappa(s)}\). By Serre's theorem the section
270	  graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded
271	  \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra, so it is Noetherian. For the coherent sheaf
272	  \(\mathcal{F}_s\), the same ampleness yields, again by Serre, that
273	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
274	  finitely generated graded module over \(R(X_s, L_s)\): the twists
275	  \(\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\) are globally generated for
276	  \(m \gg 0\), and the finitely many generating sections in low degrees together
277	  with the multiplication maps generate \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over
278	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\). Finite dimensionality of each component over \(\kappa(s)\)
279	  follows from coherence and properness (finiteness of cohomology of coherent
280	  sheaves on a proper scheme over a field).
281	\end{proof}
282	
283	\begin{lemma}[Existence and uniqueness of the Hilbert polynomial of a rational
284	  graded series]
285	  \label{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
286	  \lean{Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly}
287	  \mathlibok
288	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
289	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Polynomial.HilbertPoly}).}
290	  Let \(F\) be a field of characteristic zero, let \(p \in F[X]\), and let
291	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then there is a unique polynomial \(h \in F[X]\) such that
292	  for some \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) and all \(n > N\) the coefficient of \(X^n\) in the
293	  power-series expansion of \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d} \in F\llbracket X\rrbracket\)
294	  equals \(h(n)\):
295	  \[
296	    \exists!\, h \in F[X],\ \exists N,\ \forall n > N : \;
297	    \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1-X)^{-d} \bigr) \;=\; h(n).
298	  \]
299	  Equivalently, the eventual coefficient function of any rational power series
300	  with denominator a power of \(1 - X\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), given by a unique
301	  polynomial \(h\) (Mathlib's \(\mathrm{hilbertPoly}\,p\,d\)). The hypothesis
302	  \([\mathrm{CharZero}\,F]\) is satisfied by \(F = \mathbb{Q}\), which is the
303	  instance used to obtain \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\).
304	\end{lemma}
305	
306	\begin{lemma}[Additivity of \(\dim_\kappa\) on a short exact sequence
307	  (rank--nullity)]
308	  \label{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
309	  \lean{Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank}
310	  \mathlibok
311	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
312	  (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Dimension.RankNullity}).}
313	  Let \(\kappa\) be a field, \(V\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space and
314	  \(W \subseteq V\) a subspace. Then
315	  \[
316	    \dim_\kappa (V / W) + \dim_\kappa W \;=\; \dim_\kappa V .
317	  \]
318	  Equivalently, \(\dim_\kappa = \operatorname{finrank}_\kappa\) is additive on a
319	  short exact sequence \(0 \to W \to V \to V/W \to 0\) of finite-dimensional
320	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. This is the additivity that converts the degreewise
321	  short exact sequences of graded components into additive relations among the
322	  coefficients of the Hilbert series.
323	\end{lemma}
324	
325	\begin{lemma}[The rational power series \((1 - X)^{-d}\)]
326	  \label{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
327	  \lean{PowerSeries.invOneSubPow}
328	  \mathlibok
329	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
330	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PowerSeries.WellKnown}).}
331	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). Then \((1 - X)^d\) is a
332	  unit in the power series ring \(S\llbracket X\rrbracket\), and Mathlib names its
333	  inverse
334	  \[
335	    \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(S, d) \;=\; (1 - X)^{-d}
336	      \;\in\; S\llbracket X\rrbracket^{\times}.
337	  \]
338	  Together with the coercion
339	  \(\operatorname{toPowerSeries} : F[X] \to F\llbracket X\rrbracket\) of a
340	  polynomial to its power series, this is the data in which a rational Hilbert
341	  series \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\) is expressed; it is the same rational-series shape
342	  whose eventual coefficients are extracted by
343	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}.
344	\end{lemma}
345	
346	\begin{lemma}\leanok
347	[Graded Hilbert--Serre: rationality of the Hilbert series]
348	  \label{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}
349	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational}
350	  \uses{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib,
351	    def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero, lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq,
352	    lem:ratHilb_bump, def:graded_subquotientHilb,
353	    lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}
354	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
355	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
356	  % 00K1) (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13893-L13905).
357	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
358	  % "Suppose that $S$ is a Noetherian graded ring
359	  %  and $M$ a finite graded $S$-module. Consider the
360	  %  function
361	  %  $$
362	  %  \mathbf{Z} \longrightarrow K'_0(S_0), \quad
363	  %  n \longmapsto [M_n]
364	  %  $$
365	  %  see Lemma \ref{lemma-graded-module-fg}.
366	  %  If $S_{+}$ is generated by elements of degree $1$,
367	  %  then this function is a numerical polynomial."
368	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", Tag 00K1
369	  (proposition on graded Hilbert polynomials); cf.\ [Nitsure], \S 1 (Snapper's
370	  Lemma), [Hartshorne], I.7.}
371	  Let \(R\) be a Noetherian graded ring whose degree-zero part is a field
372	  \(\kappa\) and which is generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra by finitely many
373	  elements, and let \(M\) be a finitely generated graded \(R\)-module with
374	  finite-dimensional graded components \(M_n\). Then there exist
375	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
376	  such that for all \(n > N\)
377	  \[
378	    \dim_{\kappa} M_n \;=\; \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr);
379	  \]
380	  that is, the Hilbert series \(\sum_{n \ge 0} (\dim_\kappa M_n)\, X^n\) is, up to
381	  a polynomial correction in low degrees, the rational function
382	  \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\), expressed in the power-series form
383	  \(\operatorname{toPowerSeries}(p) \cdot \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, d)\)
384	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}).
385	
386	  \emph{Status.} This is the substantive half of the graded Hilbert--Serre
387	  theorem (rationality of the Hilbert series of a finitely generated graded
388	  module). It is not covered by Mathlib: \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
389	  supplies only the converse polynomial-extraction step from a rational series, not
390	  the production of the rational series from the module. The argument is realised by
391	  an \emph{ambient subquotient induction} (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}):
392	  the whole induction ranges over pairs of homogeneous submodules \(N' \le N\) of the
393	  single fixed graded module \(M\), and the Hilbert function is the ambient difference
394	  \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap M_n) - \dim_\kappa(N' \cap M_n)\). The class of such
395	  pairs is closed under the kernel and cokernel of multiplication by a degree-one
396	  element (each presented again as an ambient subquotient pair), so the inductive step
397	  never forms a graded quotient ring \(R/(x)\), a graded quotient module \(M/xM\), or a
398	  regrading of a derived carrier. The genuine Mathlib gap is thereby \emph{avoided}
399	  rather than filled; the residual obligation is the short ambient bookkeeping of the
400	  subquotient blocks (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb},
401	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker},
402	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff},
403	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer},
404	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}), each of which manipulates only ambient
405	  families \(n \mapsto N_{\mathrm{aux}} \cap M_n\) of submodules of \(M\).
406	  % NOTE: The power-series rationality toolkit (\cref{subsec:isRatHilb}) is formalized
407	  %   and axiom-clean. The present theorem is not yet a Lean declaration; the ambient
408	  %   subquotient route (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}) sidesteps missing
409	  %   graded-quotient API by working with ambient families of submodules of \(M\).
410	\end{lemma}
411	
412	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, inductive step (read
413	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13938-L13947).
414	% "Let $\overline{M} = M/xM$. Note that the map $x : M \to M$
415	%  is {\it not} a map of graded $S$-modules, since it does
416	%  not map $M_d$ into $M_d$. Namely, for each $d$ we have the
417	%  following short exact sequence
418	%  $$
419	%  0 \to M_d \xrightarrow{x} M_{d + 1} \to \overline{M}_{d + 1} \to 0
420	%  $$
421	%  This proves that $[M_{d + 1}] - [M_d] = [\overline{M}_{d + 1}]$.
422	%  Hence we win by Lemma \ref{lemma-numerical-polynomial}."
423	\begin{proof}
424	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero, lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq,
425	    lem:ratHilb_bump, lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff,
426	    def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
427	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
428	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb,
429	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
430	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
431	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib,
432	    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
433	  We argue with the abstract graded \(\kappa\)-algebra
434	  \(R = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} R_n\), whose degree-zero part \(R_0 = \kappa\) is a
435	  field, and the finitely generated graded \(R\)-module
436	  \(M = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} M_n\) with finite-dimensional components \(M_n\);
437	  there is no appeal to any geometric realisation of \(R\) or \(M\).
438	
439	  \emph{Reduction to generation in degree \(1\).} Since \(R\) is Noetherian and
440	  finitely generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra, it is generated by finitely many
441	  homogeneous elements of positive degree. Replacing \(R\) by a Veronese
442	  subalgebra \(R^{(e)} = \bigoplus_n R_{en}\) for a suitable \(e\) and re-grading
443	  (and \(M\) by the corresponding Veronese module), we may assume \(R\) is
444	  generated by its degree-one part, \(\kappa[R_1] = R\). The degree-one part
445	  \(R_1\) is then a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space whose \(\kappa\)-basis
446	  \(x_1, \dots, x_r\) generates \(R\) as a \(\kappa\)-algebra; \(r = \dim_\kappa R_1\)
447	  is the number on which we induct.
448	  % NOTE: The existence of finitely many degree-one generators (the number r the
449	  % induction is on) is expected to follow from the standing hypotheses --
450	  % "finitely generated κ-algebra" together with "generated in degree 1" -- so no
451	  % new hypothesis on gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational is anticipated. To be
452	  % confirmed when the prover formalizes the assembly: degree-1 generation gives
453	  % R₁ as a finite-dimensional κ-vector space spanning R as a κ-algebra, and a
454	  % κ-basis of R₁ is a finite degree-one generating set.
455	
456	  \emph{Set-up of the ambient data.} Regard \(M\) as the fixed ambient graded
457	  \(\kappa\)-module with internal decomposition \(n \mapsto \mathcal{M}_n := M_n\)
458	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}); the components are finite-dimensional. For each
459	  basis vector \(x_i \in R_1\) let \(t_i : M \to M\) be multiplication by \(x_i\), a
460	  \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism that raises degree by one (it sends \(M_n\) into
461	  \(M_{n+1}\), \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}). Since \(R\) is
462	  commutative the \(t_i\) pairwise commute, and they trivially preserve the pair of
463	  homogeneous submodules \((N, N') = (\top, \bot)\). Because \(R\) is generated by
464	  \(x_1, \dots, x_r\) and \(M\) is finitely generated over \(R\), \(M\) is finitely
465	  generated as a module over the polynomial ring \(\kappa[x_1, \dots, x_r]\) acting
466	  through the \(t_i\); this is the finiteness condition required below.
467	
468	  \emph{Invoking the ambient subquotient induction.} Apply
469	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb} to the pair \((N, N') = (\top, \bot)\)
470	  with the \(r\) commuting degree-\((+1)\) endomorphisms \(t_1, \dots, t_r\). It
471	  yields \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) of order \(r\) for the ambient subquotient Hilbert
472	  function of this pair (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}),
473	  \[
474	    h(n) \;=\; \dim_\kappa(\top \cap \mathcal{M}_n) - \dim_\kappa(\bot \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
475	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa M_n - 0 \;=\; \dim_\kappa M_n .
476	  \]
477	  Hence \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa M_n,\, r)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}):
478	  there are \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\), \(d := r \in \mathbb{N}\) and \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
479	  with
480	  \[
481	    \dim_\kappa M_n \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr)
482	    \qquad (n > N),
483	  \]
484	  which is the claim, with pole order \(d = r\). The internal mechanics of
485	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb} -- the base case via
486	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}, and the inductive step assembling the
487	  kernel/cokernel closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}), the degreewise
488	  difference identity (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}, built on
489	  \(D_5\), \cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}) and the finiteness transfer
490	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}) into
491	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq} and \cref{lem:ratHilb_bump} -- are the Stacks~00K1
492	  induction realised entirely with ambient homogeneous submodules of \(M\).
493	\end{proof}
494	
495	\subsection{The rationality toolkit \texorpdfstring{\(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\)}{IsRatHilb}}
496	\label{subsec:isRatHilb}
497	% NOTE: Declarations in this subsection are currently \texttt{private} in the Lean
498	% source; \lean{} pins may not resolve until they are moved to a dedicated module.
499	
500	The proof of \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} runs the Stacks~00K1 induction
501	on the number of degree-one generators. Each inductive step manipulates the
502	\emph{shape} of the Hilbert series -- a numerator polynomial over a power of
503	\((1-X)\) -- rather than the module itself. We isolate that bookkeeping in a
504	single predicate \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) on functions \(f : \mathbb{N} \to
505	\mathbb{Q}\), together with the closure lemmas the induction consumes. These are
506	project-internal helpers realising standard steps of the Stacks~00K1 argument at
507	the level of power series; Mathlib supplies only the converse extraction
508	(\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}).
509	
510	\begin{lemma}\leanok
511	[Coefficients of \((1-X)^{-1}\) times a series are partial sums]
512	  \label{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
513	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
514	  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
515	  Let \(F \in \mathbb{Q}\llbracket X\rrbracket\) be a power series. Then for every
516	  \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) the \(n\)-th coefficient of
517	  \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, 1)\cdot F = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\)
518	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}) is the partial sum of the coefficients of
519	  \(F\):
520	  \[
521	    \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl((1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\bigr)
522	    \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{n} \bigl[X^k\bigr] F .
523	  \]
524	  Equivalently, multiplication by \((1-X)^{-1} = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) is the
525	  summation operator on coefficient sequences. Proved directly in Lean from the
526	  identity \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q},1) = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) and
527	  the Cauchy product.
528	\end{lemma}
529	
530	\begin{lemma}\leanok
531	[Antidifference of a rational Hilbert series]
532	  \label{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
533	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalHilbert_antidiff}
534	  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
535	  Let \(H, \delta : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\), let \(q \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and
536	  \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). Suppose that for all \(n > N\) the sequence \(\delta\)
537	  is the coefficient sequence of the rational series \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\),
538	  \[
539	    \delta(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\bigr),
540	  \]
541	  and that \(\delta\) is the first difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\), i.e.
542	  \(H(n+1) - H(n) = \delta(n+1)\) for all \(n > N\). Then there is a numerator
543	  polynomial \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) such that for all \(n > N\)
544	  \[
545	    H(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr).
546	  \]
547	  That is, summing a coefficient sequence (anti-differencing) raises the pole
548	  order by one: the partial-sum series of \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\) equals
549	  \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\) up to a polynomial correction in low degrees. This is
550	  the power-series heart of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step.
551	\end{lemma}
552	
553	\begin{proof}
554	  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
555	  Write \(F = q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\). Multiplying by
556	  \((1-X)^{-1}\) and using the factorisation
557	  \((1-X)^{-(e+1)} = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\)
558	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}), \cref{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul} gives
559	  \(\bigl[X^m\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr) = \sum_{k=0}^{m}[X^k]F\), the
560	  partial sums of \(F\). On the other hand, telescoping the first-difference
561	  hypothesis from \(N+1\) expresses \(H(N+1+j)\) as \(H(N+1)\) plus a window sum
562	  of the coefficients of \(F\). Splitting the partial sum
563	  \(\sum_{k=0}^{n}[X^k]F\) at \(N+2\) and absorbing the resulting constant
564	  discrepancy into a numerator \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\) (a constant function is the
565	  order-\((e+1)\) coefficient sequence of \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)} =
566	  C\,(1-X)^{-1}\)), the explicit numerator
567	  \(p = C\,(1-X)^{e} + q\) realises \(H(n)\) as
568	  \([X^n]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr)\) for all \(n > N\).
569	\end{proof}
570	
571	\begin{definition}\leanok
572	[Rational Hilbert function]
573	  \label{def:ratHilb}
574	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb}
575	  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
576	  \textit{Source: Stacks 00K1.}
577	  For a function \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\), say
578	  that \(f\) is a \emph{rational Hilbert function of order \(d\)}, written
579	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\), when there exist a numerator polynomial
580	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) such that for all \(n > N\)
581	  \[
582	    f(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\bigr)
583	    \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(\operatorname{toPowerSeries}(p)\cdot
584	      \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, d)\bigr)
585	  \]
586	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}). This is the ``eventually equal to the
587	  coefficient sequence of a rational series \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\)'' predicate that
588	  the graded Hilbert--Serre induction tracks; its conclusion shape is exactly the
589	  one consumed by \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}.
590	\end{definition}
591	
592	\begin{lemma}\leanok
593	[Base case: eventually-zero functions are rational of order \(0\)]
594	  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}
595	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofEventuallyZero}
596	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
597	  If \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) is eventually zero -- there is
598	  \(N\) with \(f(n) = 0\) for all \(n > N\) -- then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, 0)\)
599	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}). Indeed \(f\) is then the coefficient sequence of the
600	  numerator polynomial \(0\) over \((1-X)^{0} = 1\). This is the base case of the
601	  Stacks~00K1 induction (no degree-one generators). Proved directly in Lean.
602	\end{lemma}
603	
604	\begin{lemma}\leanok
605	[Raising the pole order]
606	  \label{lem:ratHilb_bump}
607	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.bump}
608	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
609	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) then
610	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d+1)\): multiplying the numerator by \((1-X)\) cancels
611	  one factor of \((1-X)^{-1}\), so the same coefficient sequence is realised at
612	  pole order \(d+1\). This lets two rational Hilbert functions of different
613	  orders be brought to a common denominator. Proved directly in Lean using
614	  \((1-X)\cdot(1-X)^{-(d+1)} = (1-X)^{-d}\) (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}).
615	\end{lemma}
616	
617	\begin{lemma}\leanok
618	[Closure under difference]
619	  \label{lem:ratHilb_sub}
620	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.sub}
621	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
622	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, d)\)
623	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}) at the same order \(d\), then
624	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto f(n) - g(n),\, d)\): the numerator of the
625	  difference is the difference of the numerators (over the common denominator
626	  \((1-X)^{-d}\)). Proved directly in Lean.
627	\end{lemma}
628	
629	\begin{lemma}\leanok
630	[Closure under right shift]
631	  \label{lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
632	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.shiftRight}
633	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
634	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) then
635	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto f(n-1),\, d)\): the right shift on coefficient
636	  sequences is multiplication of the series by \(X\), so the numerator
637	  \(p\) is replaced by \(X\cdot p\) at the same pole order. Proved directly in
638	  Lean.
639	\end{lemma}
640	
641	\begin{lemma}\leanok
642	[Antidifference step for the predicate]
643	  \label{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}
644	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.antidiff}
645	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
646	  Let \(H, g : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). If
647	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, e)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) and \(g\) is the first
648	  difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\) (\(H(n+1) - H(n) = g(n+1)\) for all
649	  \(n > N\)), then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(H, e+1)\). This is the predicate-level
650	  packaging of \cref{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}: a finite difference raises
651	  the pole order by one, the inductive heart of graded Hilbert--Serre.
652	\end{lemma}
653	
654	\begin{proof}
655	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
656	  Unfolding \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, e)\) supplies a numerator \(q\) and a
657	  threshold \(N_g\) with \(g(n) = [X^n](q\cdot(1-X)^{-e})\) for \(n > N_g\).
658	  Applying \cref{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff} with this \(q\) and threshold
659	  \(\max(N, N_g)\) -- whose two hypotheses are exactly the rational form of \(g\)
660	  and the first-difference identity for \(H\) -- yields a numerator \(p\) with
661	  \(H(n) = [X^n](p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)})\) for \(n > \max(N, N_g)\), i.e.
662	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(H, e+1)\).
663	\end{proof}
664	
665	\begin{lemma}\leanok
666	[Inductive-step engine from a degreewise short exact sequence]
667	  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}
668	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofDiffEq}
669	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
670	  Let \(h_M, h_C, h_K : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d, N \in \mathbb{N}\).
671	  Suppose \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, d)\)
672	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}), and that for all \(n > N\)
673	  \[
674	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
675	  \]
676	  Then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\). This packages the entire power-series
677	  side of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step: \(h_M\) is the Hilbert function of
678	  \(M\), and \(h_C, h_K\) are the Hilbert functions of the cokernel
679	  \(C = M/xM\) and kernel \(K = \ker(x : M \to M(1))\) of multiplication by a
680	  degree-one element \(x\); the displayed identity is the alternating-sum
681	  consequence of the degreewise short exact sequence
682	  \(0 \to K_n \to M_n \xrightarrow{x} M_{n+1} \to C_{n+1} \to 0\). Given the
683	  inductive hypothesis that \(h_C, h_K\) are rational of order \(d\), the
684	  conclusion is rationality of \(h_M\) at order \(d+1\).
685	\end{lemma}
686	
687	\begin{proof}
688	  \uses{lem:ratHilb_antidiff, lem:ratHilb_sub, lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
689	  Set \(g(n) = h_C(n) - h_K(n-1)\). By closure under right shift
690	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}) \(n \mapsto h_K(n-1)\) is rational of order
691	  \(d\), and by closure under difference (\cref{lem:ratHilb_sub}) so is \(g\). The
692	  hypothesis rewrites as \(h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) = g(n+1)\) for \(n > N\) (since
693	  \(g(n+1) = h_C(n+1) - h_K(n)\)). Applying the antidifference step
694	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}) to \(H = h_M\) and this \(g\) gives
695	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_M, d+1)\).
696	\end{proof}
697	
698	\begin{theorem}[Hilbert polynomial from the section module]
699	  \label{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
700	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomialOfSectionModule}
701	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
702	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
703	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
704	  % Polynomials" (read from
705	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L478).
706	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
707	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
708	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
709	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
710	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$. Then for each $s\in S$,
711	  %  we get a polynomial ${\Phi}_s \in  \Q[\lambda]$
712	  %  which is the Hilbert polynomial of
713	  %  the restriction $\F_s = F|_{X_s}$ of $\F$ to the fiber $X_s$ over $s$,
714	  %  calculated with respect to the line bundle $L_s = L|_{X_s}$."
715	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
716	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
717	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).
718	  Then there is a unique polynomial \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
719	  such that for all \(m \gg 0\)
720	  \[
721	    \Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m)
722	    \;=\; \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma\bigl(X_s,\, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\bigr).
723	  \]
724	  This \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}\) is the Hilbert polynomial of
725	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}.
726	\end{theorem}
727	
728	\begin{proof}
729	  \uses{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg, lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational,
730	    lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
731	  By \cref{lem:sectionGradedModule_fg} the section graded module
732	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded module over the
733	  Noetherian graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\), with finite-dimensional components and
734	  degree-zero part the field \(\kappa(s)\). Applying the abstract graded
735	  Hilbert--Serre rationality lemma (\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) to the
736	  graded \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra \(R = R(X_s, L_s)\) and the finitely generated
737	  graded module \(M = M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) exhibits the graded Hilbert
738	  function
739	  \(m \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
740	  as the eventual coefficient function of a rational series: there are
741	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N\) with
742	  \(\dim_{\kappa(s)} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})
743	  = [X^m]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\) for \(m > N\). Apply
744	  \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib} over the characteristic-zero field
745	  \(F = \mathbb{Q}\) to this pair \((p, d)\): it yields a unique polynomial
746	  \(h \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) whose values \(h(n)\) eventually equal
747	  \([X^n]\,(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d})\). Setting
748	  \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} := h\) (with the variable renamed \(\lambda\)) gives a
749	  polynomial with \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s}(m) = \dim_{\kappa(s)}
750	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) for \(m \gg 0\). Uniqueness
751	  is the \(\exists!\) clause of \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}: any two
752	  polynomials agreeing with the Hilbert function for \(m \gg 0\) agree at
753	  infinitely many points, hence are equal.
754	\end{proof}
755	
756	\subsection{The ambient subquotient induction for the Stacks~00K1 step}
757	\label{subsec:gradedModuleApi}
758	
759	The power-series side of \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} is fully isolated
760	in the \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) toolkit (\cref{subsec:isRatHilb}). What remains is
761	the \emph{graded-module} side of the Stacks~00K1 induction. Rather than build the
762	kernel and cokernel of multiplication by a degree-one element as graded modules
763	over a smaller quotient ring \(R/(x)\) -- which would force a graded structure on a
764	derived carrier -- we range the induction over \emph{pairs of homogeneous
765	submodules of a single fixed ambient graded module} \(M\). A pair \((N, N')\) with
766	\(N' \le N\) homogeneous submodules of \(M\) stands for the subquotient \(N/N'\); its
767	ambient Hilbert function is the difference \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap M_n) -
768	\dim_\kappa(N' \cap M_n)\). The class of such pairs, equipped with finitely many
769	pairwise-commuting degree-one endomorphisms preserving \(N\) and \(N'\), is
770	\emph{closed} under the kernel and cokernel of one of those endomorphisms, both
771	again presented as ambient subquotient pairs. Consequently no graded quotient ring,
772	graded quotient module, or regrading of a subtype/quotient carrier is ever formed:
773	every object that appears is an ambient family \(n \mapsto N_{\mathrm{aux}} \cap M_n\)
774	of submodules of the fixed \(M\).
775	
776	This subsection records that machinery as a thin layer over existing Mathlib
777	scaffold: Mathlib already provides the notion of a homogeneous submodule
778	(\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}), the homogeneity of a span of
779	homogeneous elements (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib}), the degree-shift
780	of multiplication by a homogeneous element
781	(\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}), the rank--nullity identity
782	(\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}), and the finite-generation
783	transfer along a surjection (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}).
784	What is supplied here is the internal direct-sum decomposition of a homogeneous
785	submodule as an ambient family (the split \(G_1\)), the subquotient Hilbert data,
786	the kernel/cokernel closure, the degreewise difference identity, the ambient
787	finiteness transfer, and the induction itself.
788	
789	\emph{Setting for this subsection.} Let \(R = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} R_n\) be a graded
790	ring whose degree-zero part \(R_0 = \kappa\) is a field and which is generated as a
791	\(\kappa\)-algebra in degree one, and let \(M = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} M_n\) be a graded
792	\(\kappa\)-vector space (the fixed ambient module) with finite-dimensional
793	components \(M_n\). All submodules \(N, N', \dots\) below are homogeneous submodules
794	of \(M\), and the relevant endomorphisms are \(\kappa\)-linear maps \(M \to M\)
795	raising degree by one -- typically multiplication by a homogeneous element
796	\(x \in R_1\).
797	
798	\subsubsection*{Mathlib dependency anchors}
799	
800	\begin{lemma}[Homogeneous submodule]
801	  \label{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
802	  \lean{Submodule.IsHomogeneous}
803	  \mathlibok
804	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
805	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.GradedAlgebra.Homogeneous.Submodule}).}
806	  Let \(R = \bigoplus_n R_n\) be a graded ring and \(M = \bigoplus_n M_n\) an
807	  internally graded \(R\)-module. A submodule \(p \subseteq M\) is
808	  \emph{homogeneous} when it is closed under taking homogeneous components:
809	  \(m \in p\) implies each graded piece \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in p\). Equivalently
810	  \(p = \bigoplus_n (p \cap M_n)\) as a set. Mathlib bundles this into a homogeneous
811	  submodule type providing order and extensionality structure; the ambient internal
812	  direct-sum decomposition \(p = \bigoplus_n (M_n \cap p)\) is supplied by the split
813	  \(G_1\) (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep},
814	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}).
815	\end{lemma}
816	
817	\begin{lemma}[A span of homogeneous elements is homogeneous]
818	  \label{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib}
819	  \lean{Ideal.homogeneous_span}
820	  \mathlibok
821	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
822	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.GradedAlgebra.Homogeneous.Ideal}).}
823	  Let \(R = \bigoplus_n R_n\) be a graded ring and \(s \subseteq R\) a set each of
824	  whose elements is homogeneous. Then the ideal \(\mathrm{span}\,s\) is a
825	  homogeneous ideal. In particular, for \(x \in R_1\) the principal ideal \((x)\)
826	  is homogeneous, hence \(xM\) and the auxiliary submodule \(N' + x \cdot N\) are
827	  homogeneous; this homogeneity feeds the kernel/cokernel closure
828	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}).
829	\end{lemma}
830	
831	\begin{lemma}[Rank--nullity]
832	  \label{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}
833	  \lean{LinearMap.finrank_range_add_finrank_ker}
834	  \mathlibok
835	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
836	  (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.FiniteDimensional.Lemmas}).}
837	  Let \(\kappa\) be a field, \(V\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space and
838	  \(\varphi : V \to W\) a \(\kappa\)-linear map. Then
839	  \[
840	    \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) + \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)
841	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa V .
842	  \]
843	  This is the rank--nullity identity underlying the degreewise difference identity
844	  \(D_5\) (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}).
845	\end{lemma}
846	
847	\begin{lemma}[Finite generation transfers along a surjection]
848	  \label{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
849	  \lean{Submodule.FG.restrictScalars_of_surjective}
850	  \mathlibok
851	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Finiteness.Basic}).}
852	  Let \(\varphi : R \to A\) be a surjective ring homomorphism and \(M\) an
853	  \(A\)-module that is finitely generated when viewed as an \(R\)-module via
854	  \(\varphi\). Then \(M\) is finitely generated as an \(A\)-module. Applied to the
855	  surjection \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots,
856	  t_{r-2}]\) killing the endomorphism \(x = t_{r-1}\) that annihilates the two
857	  subquotients, this transfers their finite generation down one generator
858	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}).
859	\end{lemma}
860	
861	\begin{lemma}[Multiplication by a homogeneous element shifts degree]
862	  \label{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}
863	  \lean{SetLike.IsHomogeneousElem.graded_smul}
864	  \mathlibok
865	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.GradedMulAction}).}
866	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be a graded family acting on a graded family \(\mathcal{M}\)
867	  via a graded scalar multiplication. If \(a \in R\) is homogeneous of degree
868	  \(i\) -- in particular \(x \in R_1\) -- then for \(m \in M_n\) one has
869	  \(a \cdot m \in M_{i+n}\). This is the degree-shifting property that makes \(xM\)
870	  a homogeneous submodule (with degree-\((n+1)\) part \(x \cdot M_n\)) and that
871	  underlies the ambient identity \(x \cdot N \cap M_{n+1} = x \cdot (N \cap M_n)\)
872	  used in the subquotient closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}) and
873	  difference identity (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}).
874	\end{lemma}
875	
876	\begin{lemma}[Commutative subalgebra generated by commuting elements]
877	  \label{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib}
878	  \lean{Algebra.isMulCommutative_adjoin}
879	  \mathlibok
880	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
881	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Algebra.Subalgebra.Lattice}).}
882	  Let \(R\) be a commutative semiring and \(A\) an \(R\)-algebra, possibly
883	  noncommutative. If \(s \subseteq A\) is a set of pairwise-commuting elements
884	  (\(a b = b a\) for all \(a, b \in s\)), then the \(R\)-subalgebra
885	  \(\mathrm{adjoin}_R(s)\) generated by \(s\) has commutative multiplication; the
886	  commutative-ring structure on it follows. Applied to the \(r\) pairwise-commuting degree-raising endomorphisms
887	  \(x_0, \dots, x_{r-1} \in \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\), it yields a
888	  \emph{commutative} \(\kappa\)-subalgebra \(A = \mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{x_0, \dots,
889	  x_{r-1}\} \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\); the polynomial action of
890	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) on \(M\) factors through this commutative \(A\)
891	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}).
892	\end{lemma}

894	\subsubsection*{The internal grading of a homogeneous submodule (split $G_1$)}
895	
896	\begin{lemma}\leanok
897	[$G_1$ (independence) -- the family $n \mapsto M_n \cap p$ is independent]
898	  \label{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep}
899	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.homogeneousSubmodule_inf_iSupIndep}
900	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
901	  Let \(M = \bigoplus_n M_n\) be an internally graded \(\kappa\)-module and let
902	  \(p \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
903	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then the family
904	  \(n \mapsto M_n \cap p\) of \(\kappa\)-subspaces of \(M\) is independent: for each
905	  \(n\),
906	  \[
907	    (M_n \cap p) \;\cap\; \Bigl(\bigsqcup_{m \ne n} (M_m \cap p)\Bigr) \;=\; 0 .
908	  \]
909	  The whole statement lives in the ambient module \(M\): the subspaces
910	  \(M_n \cap p\) are submodules of \(M\), never of a derived carrier \(\smash{\hat p}\).
911	\end{lemma}
912	
913	\begin{proof}
914	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
915	  The components \(M_n\) of the internal decomposition of \(M\) are independent. The
916	  subfamily \(n \mapsto M_n \cap p\) consists of submodules with \(M_n \cap p \le M_n\),
917	  so independence is inherited: a relation among the \(M_n \cap p\) is in particular a
918	  relation among the \(M_n\), forcing each term to vanish.
919	\end{proof}
920	
921	\begin{lemma}\leanok
922	[$G_1$ (supremum) -- $\bigsqcup_n (M_n \cap p) = p$ for $p$ homogeneous]
923	  \label{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}
924	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_inf_eq}
925	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
926	  Let \(p \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
927	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then
928	  \[
929	    \bigsqcup_{n} (M_n \cap p) \;=\; p .
930	  \]
931	  Together with the independence of
932	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep}, this is the ambient internal
933	  direct sum \(p = \bigoplus_n (M_n \cap p)\), stated entirely inside \(M\) without
934	  requiring a direct-sum decomposition of the subtype \(\smash{\hat p}\).
935	\end{lemma}
936	
937	\begin{proof}
938	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
939	  The inclusion \(\bigsqcup_n (M_n \cap p) \le p\) is clear, since each
940	  \(M_n \cap p \le p\). For the reverse inclusion, take \(m \in p\) and write
941	  \(m = \sum_n \mathrm{proj}_n(m)\) with \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in M_n\) using the
942	  internal decomposition of \(M\). Homogeneity of \(p\) gives
943	  \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in p\), hence \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in M_n \cap p\); thus
944	  \(m\) is a finite sum of elements of the \(M_n \cap p\), i.e.
945	  \(m \in \bigsqcup_n (M_n \cap p)\).
946	\end{proof}
