948	\subsubsection*{The ambient subquotient class and its Hilbert function}
949	
950	\begin{definition}\leanok
951	[Subquotient Hilbert data]
952	  \label{def:graded_subquotientHilb}
953	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotientHilb}
954	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.SubquotientDatum}
955	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.SubquotientDatum.hilb}
956	  \uses{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
957	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}
958	  Fix a graded \(\kappa\)-module \(\mathcal{M}\), i.e.\ \(M\) together with an
959	  internal decomposition \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\) whose components
I need to continue reading the blueprint chapter. Let me read the rest of it.
Let me continue reading the blueprint chapter.

960	  \(\mathcal{M}_n\) are finite-dimensional over \(\kappa\). A \emph{subquotient
961	  datum of length \(r\)} consists of:
962	  \begin{itemize}
963	    \item a pair of homogeneous submodules \(N' \le N \subseteq M\); and
964	    \item a family \(t : \{0, \dots, r-1\} \to (M \to_\kappa M)\) of pairwise
965	      commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms, each raising degree by one
966	      (\(t_i(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\)), each preserving \(N\)
967	      and \(N'\) (\(t_i N \subseteq N\), \(t_i N' \subseteq N'\)); together with
968	    \item a finiteness condition: the subquotient \(N/N'\) is a finite module over the
969	      polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) acting through the \(t_i\) (via the
970	      ambient \(t\)-stable carriers \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,N\),
971	      \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,N'\) of \cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}).
972	  \end{itemize}
973	  The pair \((N, N')\) represents the subquotient \(N/N'\). Its \emph{ambient
974	  Hilbert function} is
975	  \[
976	    \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; \Bigl(\dim_\kappa (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
977	      \;-\; \dim_\kappa (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\Bigr) \;\in\; \mathbb{Q},
978	  \]
979	  the difference of the (finite) \(\kappa\)-dimensions of the ambient intersections,
980	  computed in \(\mathbb{Z}\) and cast to \(\mathbb{Q}\). Since \(N' \le N\) one has
981	  \(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n \le N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), so
982	  \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = \dim_\kappa\bigl((N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\bigr)\)
983	  is the dimension of the degree-\(n\) piece of \(N/N'\). The split \(G_1\)
984	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep},
985	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) guarantees that these ambient
986	  intersections assemble the homogeneous submodules \(N, N'\), so the data depends
987	  only on the ambient families \(n \mapsto N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\),
988	  \(n \mapsto N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n\).
989	\end{definition}
990	
991	\subsubsection*{Ambient homogeneity calculus}
992	
993	The kernel/cokernel closure and the degreewise difference identity rest on a small
994	calculus of how a degree-raising endomorphism interacts with the internal grading and
995	with the lattice of homogeneous submodules. Throughout, \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\)
996	is the fixed internally graded \(\kappa\)-module and \(x : M \to M\) is a
997	\(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism raising degree by one. None of these statements forms a
998	derived carrier: every object is a submodule of the fixed \(M\).
999	
1000	\begin{definition}\leanok
1001	[Degree-raising endomorphism]
1002	  \label{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1003	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.RaisesDegree}
1004	  Let \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\) be an internally graded \(\kappa\)-module. A
1005	  \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism \(x : M \to M\) \emph{raises degree by one} when it
1006	  carries each graded piece into the next: \(x(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq
1007	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) for every \(n\). This is the grading-ring-free form of
1008	  ``multiplication by a degree-one element'': it abstracts each of the \(r\)
1009	  commuting degree-one generators \(x \in R_1\) acting on \(M\) into a single
1010	  endomorphism, with no reference to the grading ring \(R\).
1011	\end{definition}
1012	
1013	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1014	[Membership form of degree-raising]
1015	  \label{lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1016	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.RaisesDegree.mem}
1017	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1018	  If \(x\) raises degree by one (\cref{def:graded_raisesDegree}) and \(m \in
1019	  \mathcal{M}_n\), then \(x m \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\).
1020	\end{lemma}
1021	
1022	\begin{proof}
1023	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1024	  Immediate from the definition: \(x m \in x(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq
1025	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\).
1026	\end{proof}
1027	
1028	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1029	[Degree-shift commutation]
1030	  \label{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1031	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.decompose_raisesDegree}
1032	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree, lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1033	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one. Then for every \(m \in M\) and every \(i\) the
1034	  degree-\((i+1)\) homogeneous component of \(x m\) equals \(x\) applied to the
1035	  degree-\(i\) component of \(m\):
1036	  \[
1037	    \mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m) \;=\; x\bigl(\mathrm{proj}_i(m)\bigr) .
1038	  \]
1039	  This load-bearing commutation says a degree-raising endomorphism shifts the
1040	  internal decomposition by exactly one slot; it is what makes preimages and images
1041	  of homogeneous submodules under \(x\) homogeneous.
1042	\end{lemma}
1043	
1044	\begin{proof}
1045	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree, lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1046	  Decompose \(m = \sum_j \mathrm{proj}_j(m)\) into its finitely many homogeneous
1047	  components and apply \(x\): \(x m = \sum_j x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m))\) with
1048	  \(x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m)) \in \mathcal{M}_{j+1}\) by
1049	  \cref{lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}. Reading off the degree-\((i+1)\) component, all
1050	  terms with \(j+1 \ne i+1\) drop out, leaving \(x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m))\).
1051	\end{proof}
1052	
1053	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1054	[Degree-raising kills the bottom]
1055	  \label{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1056	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1057	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1058	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one. Then the degree-\(0\) component of \(x m\) vanishes
1059	  for every \(m\): \(\mathrm{proj}_0(x m) = 0\).
1060	\end{lemma}
1061	
1062	\begin{proof}
1063	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1064	  Writing \(x m = \sum_j x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m))\) with each summand in
1065	  \(\mathcal{M}_{j+1}\), no summand has degree \(0\) (every \(j+1 \ge 1\)), so the
1066	  degree-\(0\) component is \(0\).
1067	\end{proof}
1068	
1069	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1070	[Preimage of a homogeneous submodule is homogeneous]
1071	  \label{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}
1072	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.comap_isHomogeneous}
1073	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1074	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N' \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
1075	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then the preimage \(x^{-1}(N') =
1076	  \{m : x m \in N'\}\) is a homogeneous submodule of \(M\).
1077	\end{lemma}
1078	
1079	\begin{proof}
1080	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1081	  Let \(m \in x^{-1}(N')\) and fix a degree \(i\). By the degree-shift commutation
1082	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}), \(x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m)) =
1083	  \mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m)\), which lies in \(N'\) because \(x m \in N'\) and \(N'\)
1084	  is homogeneous. Hence \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in x^{-1}(N')\), so \(x^{-1}(N')\) is
1085	  homogeneous.
1086	\end{proof}
1087	
1088	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1089	[Image of a homogeneous submodule is homogeneous]
1090	  \label{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1091	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_isHomogeneous}
1092	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree,
1093	    lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1094	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule.
1095	  Then the image \(x \cdot N = \{x m : m \in N\}\) is a homogeneous submodule of
1096	  \(M\), with degree-\((n+1)\) piece \(x(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) and zero
1097	  degree-\(0\) piece.
1098	\end{lemma}
1099	
1100	\begin{proof}
1101	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree,
1102	    lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1103	  Take \(x m \in x \cdot N\) with \(m \in N\) and fix a degree. In degree \(0\) the
1104	  component \(\mathrm{proj}_0(x m) = 0 \in x \cdot N\)
1105	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}). In degree \(i+1\),
1106	  \(\mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m) = x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m))\)
1107	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}); since \(N\) is homogeneous,
1108	  \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in N\), so this component lies in \(x \cdot N\). Thus every
1109	  homogeneous component of \(x m\) lies in \(x \cdot N\), which is therefore
1110	  homogeneous.
1111	\end{proof}
1112	
1113	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1114	[Intersection of homogeneous submodules is homogeneous]
1115	  \label{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}
1116	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.inf_isHomogeneous}
1117	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1118	  If \(p, q \subseteq M\) are homogeneous submodules
1119	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}) then so is their intersection \(p
1120	  \cap q\). Mathlib provides no lattice-closure lemmas for homogeneous submodules, so
1121	  this is recorded here.
1122	\end{lemma}
1123	
1124	\begin{proof}
1125	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1126	  For \(m \in p \cap q\) and any degree \(i\), homogeneity of \(p\) and of \(q\)
1127	  gives \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in p\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in q\), hence
1128	  \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in p \cap q\).
1129	\end{proof}
1130	
1131	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1132	[Sum of homogeneous submodules is homogeneous]
1133	  \label{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1134	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.sup_isHomogeneous}
1135	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1136	  If \(p, q \subseteq M\) are homogeneous submodules then so is their sum \(p + q\).
1137	\end{lemma}
1138	
1139	\begin{proof}
1140	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1141	  Write an element of \(p + q\) as \(a + b\) with \(a \in p\), \(b \in q\). For any
1142	  degree \(i\), \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a+b) = \mathrm{proj}_i(a) + \mathrm{proj}_i(b)\)
1143	  with \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a) \in p\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_i(b) \in q\) by homogeneity,
1144	  so \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a+b) \in p + q\).
1145	\end{proof}
1146	
1147	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1148	[Ambient image identity]
1149	  \label{lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq}
1150	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_inf_degree_eq}
1151	  \uses{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1152	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N \subseteq M\) be homogeneous. Then the
1153	  degree-\((n+1)\) part of the image \(x \cdot N\) is exactly \(x\) of the
1154	  degree-\(n\) part of \(N\):
1155	  \[
1156	    (x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1} \;=\; x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n) .
1157	  \]
1158	\end{lemma}
1159	
1160	\begin{proof}
1161	  \uses{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1162	  \(\supseteq\): for \(m \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), \(x m \in x \cdot N\) and \(x m
1163	  \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) since \(x\) raises degree. \(\subseteq\): an element \(x m
1164	  \in x \cdot N\) lying in \(\mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) equals its own degree-\((n+1)\)
1165	  component \(\mathrm{proj}_{n+1}(x m) = x(\mathrm{proj}_n(m))\)
1166	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}); homogeneity of \(N\) puts
1167	  \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), so \(x m \in x \cdot (N \cap
1168	  \mathcal{M}_n)\). The image \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous by
1169	  \cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}.
1170	\end{proof}
1171	
1172	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1173	[Ambient distributive law]
1174	  \label{lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1175	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.sup_inf_degree_eq}
1176	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous,
1177	    lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1178	  For homogeneous submodules \(P, Q \subseteq M\) and any degree \(k\), intersecting
1179	  the sum with a graded piece distributes:
1180	  \[
1181	    (P + Q) \cap \mathcal{M}_k \;=\; (P \cap \mathcal{M}_k) + (Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k) .
1182	  \]
1183	\end{lemma}
1184	
1185	\begin{proof}
1186	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous,
1187	    lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1188	  \(\supseteq\) is the general lattice inequality. For \(\subseteq\), take \(z = p +
1189	  q \in (P + Q) \cap \mathcal{M}_k\) with \(p \in P\), \(q \in Q\). Since \(z \in
1190	  \mathcal{M}_k\), \(z = \mathrm{proj}_k(z) = \mathrm{proj}_k(p) +
1191	  \mathrm{proj}_k(q)\); homogeneity of \(P\) and \(Q\)
1192	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}) puts \(\mathrm{proj}_k(p) \in P \cap
1193	  \mathcal{M}_k\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_k(q) \in Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k\), so \(z \in (P
1194	  \cap \mathcal{M}_k) + (Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k)\). The intersection and sum stay in
1195	  the homogeneous lattice by \cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous} and
1196	  \cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}.
1197	\end{proof}
1198	
1199	\subsubsection*{Closure under kernel and cokernel}
1200	
1201	The kernel/cokernel closure of the subquotient class is realised by eight ambient
1202	component facts: for a degree-raising endomorphism \(x\) and a homogeneous pair
1203	\(N' \le N\), the kernel pair is \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and the cokernel pair is
1204	\((N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\). The lemmas below record that both new pairs are homogeneous,
1205	nest correctly, are annihilated by \(x\), and are preserved by any endomorphism \(t\)
1206	commuting with \(x\) that preserves the original pair.
1207	
1208	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1209	[Homogeneity of the kernel pair's lower module]
1210	  \label{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous}
1211	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_isHomogeneous}
1212	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}
1213	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one (\cref{def:graded_raisesDegree}) and let \(N, N'\) be
1214	  homogeneous submodules of \(M\). Then the kernel pair's lower module
1215	  \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous. Indeed it is the intersection
1216	  (\cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}) of the homogeneous \(N\) with the homogeneous
1217	  preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) (\cref{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}).
1218	\end{lemma}
1219	
1220	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1221	[Homogeneity of the cokernel pair's upper module]
1222	  \label{lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous}
1223	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_isHomogeneous}
1224	  \uses{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1225	  Under the same hypotheses, the cokernel pair's upper module \(N' + x \cdot N\) is
1226	  homogeneous: it is the sum (\cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}) of the homogeneous
1227	  \(N'\) with the homogeneous image \(x \cdot N\)
1228	  (\cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}).
1229	\end{lemma}
1230	
1231	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1232	[The kernel pair nests]
1233	  \label{lem:graded_ker_le}
1234	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_le}
1235	  If \(N' \le N\) and \(x\) preserves \(N'\) (\(x \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)), then
1236	  \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\). Indeed \(N' \le N\) by hypothesis, and \(x \cdot N'
1237	  \subseteq N'\) is exactly \(N' \subseteq x^{-1}(N')\); so \(N'\) is below the
1238	  intersection. Hence the kernel pair \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) is a valid
1239	  subquotient pair (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}).
1240	\end{lemma}
1241	
1242	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1243	[The cokernel pair nests]
1244	  \label{lem:graded_coker_le}
1245	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_le}
1246	  If \(N' \le N\) and \(x\) preserves \(N\) (\(x \cdot N \subseteq N\)), then
1247	  \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\): both summands lie in \(N\). Hence the cokernel pair
1248	  \((N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) is a valid subquotient pair
1249	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}).
1250	\end{lemma}
1251	
1252	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1253	[$x$ annihilates the kernel subquotient]
1254	  \label{lem:graded_ker_annihilate}
1255	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_annihilate}
1256	  The endomorphism \(x\) carries the kernel pair's lower module into \(N'\):
1257	  \(x \cdot (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \subseteq N'\). Indeed \(N \cap x^{-1}(N') \subseteq
1258	  x^{-1}(N')\), and applying \(x\) to a preimage of \(N'\) lands in \(N'\). Thus \(x\)
1259	  acts as zero on the kernel subquotient.
1260	\end{lemma}
1261	
1262	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1263	[$x$ annihilates the cokernel subquotient]
1264	  \label{lem:graded_coker_annihilate}
1265	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_annihilate}
1266	  The endomorphism \(x\) carries \(N\) into the cokernel pair's upper module:
1267	  \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\), trivially (the right summand). Thus \(x\)
1268	  acts as zero on the cokernel subquotient \(N/(N' + x \cdot N)\).
1269	\end{lemma}
1270	
1271	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1272	[Commuting endomorphisms preserve the preimage]
1273	  \label{lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute}
1274	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.comap_map_le_of_commute}
1275	  Let \(t\) commute with \(x\) and preserve \(N'\) (\(t \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)). Then
1276	  \(t\) preserves the preimage: \(t \cdot x^{-1}(N') \subseteq x^{-1}(N')\). Indeed for
1277	  \(m\) with \(x m \in N'\) one has \(x(t m) = t(x m) \in t \cdot N' \subseteq N'\), so
1278	  \(t m \in x^{-1}(N')\). This is what shows the remaining \(t_i\) preserve the kernel
1279	  pair's lower module \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\).
1280	\end{lemma}
1281	
1282	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1283	[Commuting endomorphisms preserve the image]
1284	  \label{lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute}
1285	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_map_le_of_commute}
1286	  Let \(t\) commute with \(x\) and preserve \(N\) (\(t \cdot N \subseteq N\)). Then
1287	  \(t\) preserves the image: \(t \cdot (x \cdot N) \subseteq x \cdot N\). Indeed for
1288	  \(m \in N\) one has \(t(x m) = x(t m)\) with \(t m \in N\), so \(t(x m) \in x \cdot
1289	  N\). This is what shows the remaining \(t_i\) preserve the cokernel pair's upper
1290	  module \(N' + x \cdot N\).
1291	\end{lemma}
1292	
1293	\begin{lemma}[Kernel/cokernel closure of the subquotient class]
1294	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}
1295	  % This is a narrative grouping lemma: its content is realised in Lean by the eight
1296	  % ambient component facts cited in \uses{} below (no single Lean declaration),
1297	  % together with the SubquotientDatum.ker/.coker constructors that bundle them into a
1298	  % length-(r-1) datum.
1299	  \uses{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous,
1300	    lem:graded_ker_le, lem:graded_coker_le, lem:graded_ker_annihilate,
1301	    lem:graded_coker_annihilate, lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute,
1302	    lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute,
1303	    def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1304	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
1305	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous,
1306	    lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous,
1307	    lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1308	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with
1309	  endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\), and set \(x = t_{r-1}\). Form the two new
1310	  pairs of homogeneous submodules of \(M\)
1311	  \[
1312	    K \;=\; \bigl(\,N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\ N'\,\bigr),
1313	    \qquad
1314	    C \;=\; \bigl(\,N,\ N' + x \cdot N\,\bigr),
1315	  \]
1316	  where \(x^{-1}(N') = \{\,m \in M : x \cdot m \in N'\,\}\) is the preimage and
1317	  \(x \cdot N\) is the image of \(N\) under \(x\). Then:
1318	  \begin{enumerate}
1319	    \item \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) and \(N' + x \cdot N\) are homogeneous submodules of
1320	      \(M\), with \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) and \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\), so both
1321	      \(K\) and \(C\) are subquotient pairs;
1322	    \item the remaining endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}\) pairwise commute,
1323	      raise degree by one, and preserve both members of \(K\) and of \(C\); and
1324	    \item \(x\) annihilates both subquotients: \(x \cdot (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \subseteq N'\)
1325	      and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\).
1326	  \end{enumerate}
1327	  Consequently \(K\) and \(C\) are again subquotient data, now of length \(r-1\)
1328	  (the endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}\)), and \(K = \ker x\) and \(C =
1329	  \operatorname{coker} x\) on the subquotient \(N/N'\): \(K\) represents
1330	  \(\ker\bigl(x : N/N' \to N/N'\bigr)\) and \(C\) represents
1331	  \(\operatorname{coker}\bigl(x : N/N' \to N/N'\bigr)\). No quotient or subtype
1332	  carrier is constructed; \(K\) and \(C\) are ambient pairs of homogeneous
1333	  submodules of the fixed \(M\).
1334	\end{lemma}
1335	
1336	\begin{proof}
1337	  \uses{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous,
1338	    lem:graded_ker_le, lem:graded_coker_le, lem:graded_ker_annihilate,
1339	    lem:graded_coker_annihilate, lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute,
1340	    lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute,
1341	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1342	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
1343	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous,
1344	    lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous,
1345	    lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1346	  \emph{Homogeneity.} The preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous: if
1347	  \(m = \sum_n \mathrm{proj}_n(m)\) with \(x \cdot m \in N'\), then since \(x\) raises
1348	  degree by one (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}) the components
1349	  \(x \cdot \mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) lie in distinct degrees, and
1350	  homogeneity of \(N'\) (split \(G_1\),
1351	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) forces each
1352	  \(x \cdot \mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in N'\), i.e.\ \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in x^{-1}(N')\).
1353	  Thus \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) is an intersection of homogeneous submodules, hence
1354	  homogeneous. The image \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous with degree-\((n+1)\) piece
1355	  \(x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) by
1356	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}, and a sum of homogeneous
1357	  submodules is homogeneous (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib} applied at the
1358	  module level), so \(N' + x \cdot N\) is homogeneous. The inclusions
1359	  \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (as \(x \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)) and
1360	  \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\) (as \(N' \le N\) and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N\)) are
1361	  immediate. In the language of the ambient homogeneity calculus, this paragraph is
1362	  exactly: \(x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous (\cref{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}),
1363	  \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous (\cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}), and the
1364	  homogeneous lattice is closed under intersection
1365	  (\cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}) and sum
1366	  (\cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}).
1367	
1368	  \emph{Preservation.} Each \(t_i\) with \(i \le r-2\) commutes with \(x\) and
1369	  preserves \(N, N'\); hence it preserves \(x^{-1}(N')\) (if \(x t_i m = t_i x m \in
1370	  N'\) whenever \(x m \in N'\)), so it preserves \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\), and it
1371	  preserves \(x \cdot N\) (as \(t_i x N = x t_i N \subseteq x N\)), so it preserves
1372	  \(N' + x \cdot N\). Degree-raising and commutativity are inherited.
1373	
1374	  \emph{Annihilation by \(x\).} For \(m \in N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) one has
1375	  \(x \cdot m \in N'\) by definition, so \(x\) carries the first member of \(K\) into
1376	  its second; and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\) trivially. Thus \(x\) acts as
1377	  zero on \(N/N'\) restricted to \(K\) and on the cokernel quotient \(C\).
1378	
1379	  \emph{Identification with \(\ker x\) and \(\operatorname{coker} x\).} On the
1380	  subquotient \(N/N'\), the class of \(m \in N\) is killed by \(x\) iff
1381	  \(x \cdot m \in N'\), i.e.\ iff \(m \in N \cap x^{-1}(N')\); so \(\ker(x : N/N' \to
1382	  N/N')\) is exactly \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'))/N'\), represented by \(K\). The image of
1383	  \(x\) on \(N/N'\) is \((N' + x \cdot N)/N'\), so the cokernel
1384	  \((N/N')/\operatorname{im} x = N/(N' + x \cdot N)\) is represented by \(C\).
1385	\end{proof}
1386	
1387	\subsubsection*{The subquotient degreewise difference identity ($D_6$)}
1388	
1389	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1390	[$D_6$ -- subquotient degreewise difference]
1391	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}
1392	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_degreewise_diff}
1393	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1394	    lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff, lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1395	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq,
1396	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq,
1397	    lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1398	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
1399	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
1400	  % 00K1), proof (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13939-L13947).
1401	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (degreewise short exact sequence).}
1402	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with
1403	  Hilbert function \(h_M\), and let \(x = t_{r-1}\). Let \(h_K\) and \(h_C\) be the
1404	  Hilbert functions of the kernel and cokernel subquotients
1405	  \(K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and \(C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) of
1406	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}. Then for every \(n\)
1407	  \[
1408	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
1409	  \]
1410	  Writing \(Q_n = (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) for the
1411	  degree-\(n\) piece of \(N/N'\) (so \(\dim_\kappa Q_n = h_M(n)\)), multiplication by
1412	  \(x\) is a \(\kappa\)-linear map \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\) with
1413	  \(\ker(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\) the degree-\(n\) piece of \(K\) and
1414	  \(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im} x\) the degree-\((n+1)\) piece of \(C\); the identity is
1415	  then the degreewise rank--nullity difference \(D_5\)
1416	  (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}). Specialising to \((N, N') = (\top,
1417	  \bot)\) recovers the classical \(0 \to \mathcal{M}_n \xrightarrow{x}
1418	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1} \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1}/x\mathcal{M}_n \to 0\).
1419	\end{lemma}
1420	
1421	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, inductive step (read
1422	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13939-L13947).
1423	% "Let $\overline{M} = M/xM$. Note that the map $x : M \to M$
1424	%  is {\it not} a map of graded $S$-modules, since it does
1425	%  not map $M_d$ into $M_d$. Namely, for each $d$ we have the
1426	%  following short exact sequence
1427	%  $$
1428	%  0 \to M_d \xrightarrow{x} M_{d + 1} \to \overline{M}_{d + 1} \to 0
1429	%  $$
1430	%  This proves that $[M_{d + 1}] - [M_d] = [\overline{M}_{d + 1}]$.
1431	%  Hence we win by Lemma \ref{lemma-numerical-polynomial}."
1432	\begin{proof}
1433	  \uses{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker, lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff,
1434	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq,
1435	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq,
1436	    lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1437	  Since \(N' \le N\) are homogeneous, the split \(G_1\)
1438	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) identifies the degree-\(n\) piece
1439	  of \(N/N'\) with \(Q_n = (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\), a
1440	  finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space of dimension \(h_M(n)\). Because \(x\)
1441	  raises degree by one and preserves \(N\) and \(N'\)
1442	  (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}), it induces a \(\kappa\)-linear
1443	  map \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\).
1444	
1445	  \emph{Kernel.} A class \([m] \in Q_n\) (with \(m \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\)) lies in
1446	  \(\ker(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\) iff \(x \cdot m \in N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\), i.e.\
1447	  iff \(m \in (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \cap \mathcal{M}_n\). Hence \(\ker(x : Q_n \to
1448	  Q_{n+1}) = \bigl((N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \cap \mathcal{M}_n\bigr)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1449	  is exactly the degree-\(n\) piece of \(K\), of dimension \(h_K(n)\)
1450	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}).
1451	
1452	  \emph{Cokernel.} The image of \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\) is
1453	  \(\bigl(x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n) + (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\bigr)/(N' \cap
1454	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\). Using the ambient identity \((N' + x \cdot N) \cap
1455	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1} = (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}) + x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1456	  -- which is the ambient distributive law (\cref{lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq})
1457	  applied to \(P = N'\), \(Q = x \cdot N\), combined with the ambient image identity
1458	  \((x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1} = x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1459	  (\cref{lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq}) -- the cokernel \(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im} x = (N \cap
1460	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1})/\bigl((N' + x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\bigr)\) is the
1461	  degree-\((n+1)\) piece of \(C\), of dimension \(h_C(n+1)\).
1462	
1463	  \emph{Difference.} Apply the degreewise rank--nullity difference \(D_5\)
1464	  (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}) to \(\varphi = (x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\),
1465	  with \(V = Q_n\), \(W = Q_{n+1}\):
1466	  \[
1467	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n)
1468	      = \dim_\kappa Q_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa Q_n
1469	      = \dim_\kappa(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im}\varphi) - \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)
1470	      = h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
1471	  \]
1472	  This is the \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) hypothesis consumed by \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}.
1473	\end{proof}
1474	
1475	\subsubsection*{The free polynomial-ring module structure}
1476	
1477	The finiteness condition of a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) and
1478	its transfer down one generator (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}) are
1479	phrased over the \emph{free} polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] =
1480	\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\{0, \dots, r-1\}, \kappa)\). The following blocks build the
1481	action of that ring on the ambient module \(M\) from the \(r\) pairwise-commuting
1482	degree-raising endomorphisms \(t_i\), keeping every carrier an ambient submodule of
1483	\(M\). The free polynomial ring -- rather than the subalgebra
1484	\(\mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\} \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\)
1485	-- is used precisely so the inductive transfer has the ring surjection
1486	\(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]\) available
1487	(\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}).
1488	
1489	\begin{definition}\leanok
1490	[Polynomial evaluation ring homomorphism]
1491	  \label{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1492	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom}
1493	  \uses{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib}
1494	  Let \(t : \{0, \dots, r-1\} \to \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) be a family of
1495	  pairwise-commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms. The \emph{polynomial evaluation
1496	  ring homomorphism}
1497	  \[
1498	    \mathrm{polyEndHom}(t) : \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \longrightarrow
1499	      \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)
1500	  \]
1501	  sends each variable \(X_i\) to \(t_i\). It is constructed by evaluating into the
1502	  \emph{commutative} \(\kappa\)-subalgebra \(A = \mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa(\{t_i\})
1503	  \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) -- commutative by
1504	  \cref{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib} because the generators commute -- and then
1505	  including \(A \hookrightarrow \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\); evaluation into the
1506	  noncommutative \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) directly is not an algebra
1507	  homomorphism, which is why the commutative \(A\) is interposed.
1508	\end{definition}
1509	
1510	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1511	[Evaluation sends $X_i$ to $t_i$]
1512	  \label{lem:graded_polyEndHom_X}
1513	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom_X}
1514	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1515	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(X_i) = t_i\) for each \(i\). Immediate from the construction
1516	  (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}), as evaluation sends \(X_i\) to the chosen image
1517	  \(t_i\).
1518	\end{lemma}
1519	
1520	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1521	[Evaluation sends a constant to a scalar]
1522	  \label{lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}
1523	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom_C}
1524	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1525	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(C\,c) = c \cdot \mathrm{id}_M\) for each \(c \in \kappa\):
1526	  the evaluation homomorphism (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}) is \(\kappa\)-linear and
1527	  carries the constant polynomial \(C\,c\) to the scalar endomorphism \(c \cdot 1\).
1528	\end{lemma}
1529	
1530	\begin{definition}\leanok
1531	[Polynomial-ring module structure on $M$]
1532	  \label{def:graded_polyModule}
1533	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyModule}
1534	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1535	  The \emph{polynomial-module structure} is the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-module
1536	  structure on \(M\) obtained by restricting scalars along
1537	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)\) (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}): a polynomial \(p\) acts on
1538	  \(m \in M\) by \(p \cdot m = \mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(p)(m)\). This is the module over the
1539	  free polynomial ring on which the ambient subquotient induction's finiteness condition
1540	  is measured.
1541	\end{definition}
1542	
1543	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1544	[$X_i$ acts as $t_i$]
1545	  \label{lem:graded_polyModule_X_smul}
1546	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyModule_X_smul}
1547	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, lem:graded_polyEndHom_X}
1548	  In the polynomial-module structure (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}), \(X_i \cdot m =
1549	  t_i(m)\) for all \(m \in M\). Immediate from
1550	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(X_i) = t_i\) (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_X}).
1551	\end{lemma}
1552	
1553	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1554	[A constant acts as a scalar]
1555	  \label{lem:graded_polyModule_C_smul}
1556	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyModule_C_smul}
1557	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}
1558	  In the polynomial-module structure (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}), \((C\,c) \cdot m =
1559	  c \cdot m\) for all \(c \in \kappa\), \(m \in M\). Immediate from
1560	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(C\,c) = c \cdot \mathrm{id}_M\)
1561	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}).
1562	\end{lemma}
1563	
1564	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1565	[$\kappa$-scalar tower]
1566	  \label{lem:graded_polyModule_isScalarTower}
1567	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyModule_isScalarTower}
1568	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}
1569	  The polynomial-module structure (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}) is compatible with the
1570	  original \(\kappa\)-action: \(\mathrm{IsScalarTower}\,\kappa\,\kappa[t_0, \dots,
1571	  t_{r-1}]\,M\). Indeed the algebra map \(\kappa \to \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)
1572	  followed by the polynomial action recovers the \(\kappa\)-action, since a scalar
1573	  \(c \in \kappa\) maps to the constant \(C\,c\), which acts as \(c\)
1574	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_C}).
1575	\end{lemma}
1576	
1577	\begin{definition}\leanok
1578	[Polynomial-ring submodule from a $t$-stable submodule]
1579	  \label{def:graded_polySubmodule}
1580	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polySubmodule}
1581	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, lem:graded_polyModule_X_smul,
1582	    lem:graded_polyModule_C_smul}
1583	  Let \(N \subseteq M\) be a \(\kappa\)-submodule that is stable under each \(t_i\)
1584	  (\(t_i \cdot N \subseteq N\)). Then \(N\), with the \emph{same} ambient carrier, is a
1585	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-submodule of \(M\) for the polynomial-module structure
1586	  (\cref{def:graded_polyModule}). Stability under the action of an arbitrary polynomial
1587	  follows by induction on monomials: constants act as scalars
1588	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyModule_C_smul}) and multiplication by \(X_i\) acts as \(t_i\)
1589	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyModule_X_smul}), both of which preserve \(N\).
1590	\end{definition}
1591	
1592	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1593	[The polynomial submodule has the same carrier]
1594	  \label{lem:graded_polySubmodule_coe}
1595	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polySubmodule_coe}
1596	  \uses{def:graded_polySubmodule}
1597	  The underlying set of \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}(t, N)\)
1598	  (\cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}) equals \(N\): the lift to a
1599	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-submodule does not change the carrier, so every object
1600	  in the induction remains an ambient submodule of the fixed \(M\).
1601	\end{lemma}
1602	
1603	\subsubsection*{Ambient finiteness transfer}
1604	
1605	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1606	[Finiteness transfer for the subquotients]
1607	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}
1608	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_finite_transfer}
1609	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1610	    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib, lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib,
1611	    def:graded_polyEndHom, def:graded_polyModule, def:graded_polySubmodule,
1612	    lem:graded_polyModule_isScalarTower}
1613	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum of length \(r \ge 1\)
1614	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}), so that \(M\) is finite over
1615	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\), and let \(x = t_{r-1}\). Then the two
1616	  subquotients
1617	  \[
1618	    K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N'), \qquad C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)
1619	  \]
1620	  of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker} are finite modules over
1621	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]\); that is, they are subquotient data of length
1622	  \(r-1\), satisfying the finiteness condition. This is what is needed for the
1623	  inductive hypothesis of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}.
1624	
1625	  The kernel \(K\) and cokernel \(C\) of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}
1626	  satisfy the same conditions as the original datum -- a homogeneous pair, pairwise-commuting
1627	  degree-raising endomorphisms preserving the pair, a polynomial-ring module structure, and
1628	  the finiteness condition -- but of length \(r-1\) rather than \(r\).
1629	\end{lemma}
1630	
1631	\begin{proof}
1632	  \uses{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1633	    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib, lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib,
1634	    def:graded_polyEndHom, def:graded_polyModule, def:graded_polySubmodule,
1635	    lem:graded_polyModule_isScalarTower}
1636	  \emph{The polynomial module structure on \(M\).} The \(r\) pairwise-commuting
1637	  degree-raising endomorphisms \(x_0, \dots, x_{r-1}\) generate a \(\kappa\)-subalgebra
1638	  \(A = \mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{x_0, \dots, x_{r-1}\}\) of the \emph{noncommutative}
1639	  endomorphism algebra \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\); because the generators
1640	  commute pairwise, \(A\) is commutative (\cref{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib}).
1641	  Commutativity of \(A\) is exactly what makes the evaluation homomorphism at the
1642	  generators a legal \(\kappa\)-algebra homomorphism \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]
1643	  \to A\): evaluation refuses the noncommutative \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) as a
1644	  target. Composing with the inclusion \(A \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\)
1645	  and restricting scalars along it endows \(M\) with the structure of a module over
1646	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\), compatible with the \(\kappa\)-action and
1647	  characterised by \(t_i \cdot m = x_i m\).
1648	
1649	  \emph{Transfer down one generator.} Set \(A = \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) and
1650	  \(x = t_{r-1}\). Since \(M\) is finite over the Noetherian ring \(A\), \(M\) is a
1651	  Noetherian \(A\)-module, so the submodule \(N \cap x^{-1}(N') \subseteq M\) is
1652	  finite over \(A\) and the quotients \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'))/N'\) and
1653	  \(N/(N' + x \cdot N)\) are finite over \(A\). By
1654	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker} the element \(x = t_{r-1}\) annihilates
1655	  both subquotients, so the \(A\)-action on each factors through the surjection of
1656	  polynomial rings
1657	  \[
1658	    A = \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]
1659	  \]
1660	  fixing \(t_i\) for \(i < r-1\) and sending \(t_{r-1} \mapsto 0\). Applying the
1661	  finite-generation transfer along a surjection
1662	  (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}) turns finiteness over \(A\)
1663	  into finiteness over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]\), supplying the length-\((r-1)\)
1664	  finiteness witnesses for \(K\) and \(C\).
1665	
1666	  \emph{Why the free polynomial ring.} The smaller ring must be the \emph{free}
1667	  polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]\), not the subalgebra
1668	  \(\mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{x_0, \dots, x_{r-2}\} \subseteq
1669	  \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\): relations among the endomorphisms inside
1670	  \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) would obstruct the polynomial-ring surjection that
1671	  the scalar transfer requires. Keeping free polynomial rings at every step keeps the
1672	  surjection -- and hence the transfer -- available throughout. Everything is finite
1673	  generation of ambient submodules of the fixed \(M\) and their quotients.
1674	\end{proof}
1675	
1676	\subsubsection*{The degreewise difference identity}
1677	
1678	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1679	[$D_5$ -- degreewise rank--nullity difference]
1680	  \label{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}
1681	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.degreewise_finrank_diff}
1682	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
1683	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
1684	  Let \(\varphi : V \to W\) be a \(\kappa\)-linear map between finite-dimensional
1685	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. Then
1686	  \[
1687	    \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V
1688	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) - \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi).
1689	  \]
1690	  Applied degreewise with \(\varphi = (x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\),
1691	  \(V = \mathcal{M}_n\), \(W = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) -- so that \(\ker\varphi\) is the
1692	  degree-\(n\) piece \(K_n\) of the kernel subquotient and
1693	  \(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}/x \mathcal{M}_n\) the
1694	  degree-\((n+1)\) piece \(C_{n+1}\) of the cokernel subquotient
1695	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}, via the split \(G_1\)
1696	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq} and the degree shift
1697	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}) -- this gives, for every \(n\),
1698	  \[
1699	    \dim_\kappa \mathcal{M}_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa \mathcal{M}_n
1700	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa C_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa K_n,
1701	  \]
1702	  which is the difference identity \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) consumed by
1703	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}. No graded structure is used in proving the
1704	  underlying vector-space identity -- it is pure rank--nullity.
1705	\end{lemma}
1706	
1707	\begin{proof}
1708	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
1709	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
1710	  By rank--nullity (\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}),
1711	  \(\dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi) + \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}
1712	  \varphi)\), so \(\dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa V -
1713	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). By additivity on the short exact sequence
1714	  \(0 \to \operatorname{range}\varphi \to W \to W/\operatorname{range}\varphi \to 0\)
1715	  (\cref{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}),
1716	  \(\dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W -
1717	  \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V +
1718	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). Rearranging gives
1719	  \(\dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) -
1720	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). The degreewise application identifies
1721	  \(\ker(x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\) and
1722	  \(\mathcal{M}_{n+1}/\operatorname{range}(x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\)
1723	  with the degree-\(n\) piece of the kernel subquotient and the degree-\((n+1)\)
1724	  piece of the cokernel subquotient (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}),
1725	  the latter because the degree-\((n+1)\) graded piece of the homogeneous submodule
1726	  \(x\mathcal{M}\) is exactly \(x \mathcal{M}_n\)
1727	  (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}).
1728	\end{proof}
1729	
1730	\subsubsection*{The base case ($r = 0$)}
1731	
1732	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1733	[Base-case finiteness over the empty polynomial ring]
1734	  \label{lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
1735	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
1736	  Let \(Q\) be a \(\kappa\)-vector space that is also a module over
1737	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\sigma, \kappa)\) for an \emph{empty} index set
1738	  \(\sigma\) -- in particular \(\sigma = \{0, \dots, r-1\}\) with \(r = 0\) -- in a way
1739	  compatible with the \(\kappa\)-action (scalar tower). If \(Q\) is module-finite over
1740	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\sigma, \kappa)\), then \(Q\) is finite-dimensional over
1741	  \(\kappa\). Indeed for empty \(\sigma\) the polynomial ring is
1742	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\sigma, \kappa) \cong \kappa\), a finite \(\kappa\)-module,
1743	  so finiteness of \(Q\) over it transfers to finiteness over \(\kappa\) along the scalar
1744	  tower. This is the length-zero input to the ambient subquotient induction
1745	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}): when \(r = 0\) the subquotient \(N/N'\) is a
1746	  finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space, hence \(\mathrm{hilb}\) is eventually zero.
1747	\end{lemma}
1748	
1749	\subsubsection*{The ambient subquotient induction}
1750	
1751	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1752	[Rationality of the ambient subquotient Hilbert function]
1753	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}
1754	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_hilbertSeries_rational}
1755	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1756	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
1757	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero,
1758	    lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq, lem:ratHilb_bump,
1759	    lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
1760	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
1761	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
1762	  % 00K1), proof (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13908-L13914).
1763	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (induction on the number of
1764	  degree-one generators).}
1765	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum of length \(r\)
1766	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with ambient Hilbert function
1767	  \(\mathrm{hilb}\). Then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, r)\)
1768	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}): the difference \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
1769	  - \dim_\kappa(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), the coefficient
1770	  sequence of a rational series \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-r}\) with \(p \in
1771	  \mathbb{Q}[X]\). The induction is on the length \(r\), and every object that
1772	  appears is an ambient pair of homogeneous submodules of the fixed graded module
1773	  \(M\).
1774	\end{lemma}
1775	
1776	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, induction set-up (read
1777	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13908-L13914).
1778	% "We prove this by induction on the minimal number of
1779	%  generators of $S_1$. If this number is $0$, then
1780	%  $M_n = 0$ for all $n \gg 0$ and the result holds.
1781	%  To prove the induction step, let $x\in S_1$
1782	%  be one of a minimal set of generators, such that
1783	%  the induction hypothesis applies to the
1784	%  graded ring $S/(x)$."
1785	\begin{proof}
1786	  \uses{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1787	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
1788	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero,
1789	    lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq, lem:ratHilb_bump,
1790	    lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
1791	  We induct on the length \(r\).
1792	
1793	  \emph{Base case \(r = 0\).} With no endomorphisms, the finiteness witness says
1794	  \(M\) is a finite module over \(\kappa[\,] = \kappa\), so \(M\) is a
1795	  finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space. Hence \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n = 0\) and
1796	  \(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n = 0\) for all \(n \gg 0\), so \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = 0\) for
1797	  \(n \gg 0\). By \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero} an eventually-zero function is
1798	  rational of order \(0\), giving \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, 0)\).
1799	
1800	  \emph{Inductive step \(r \ge 1\).} Set \(x = t_{r-1}\). By the kernel/cokernel
1801	  closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}) the kernel subquotient
1802	  \(K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and the cokernel subquotient
1803	  \(C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) are subquotient data of length \(r-1\), with valid
1804	  finiteness witnesses supplied by the finiteness transfer
1805	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}). The induction hypothesis applied
1806	  to \(K\) and \(C\) gives \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, r-1)\) and
1807	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, r-1)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}). By the degreewise
1808	  difference identity (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}),
1809	  \[
1810	    \mathrm{hilb}(n+1) - \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n)
1811	    \qquad \text{for all } n .
1812	  \]
1813	  Feeding \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, r-1)\), \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, r-1)\) and this
1814	  difference identity into the inductive-step engine
1815	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}) yields \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, r)\). (The
1816	  common order \(r-1\) for \(h_C\) and \(h_K\) is arranged, if necessary, by raising
1817	  the lower one with \cref{lem:ratHilb_bump}.) This completes the induction.
1818	\end{proof}

1820	\section{Support and freeness predicates}
1821	\label{sec:quot_predicates}
1822	
1823	The Quot functor (\cref{def:quot_functor}) and the Grassmannian
1824	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) classify quotient sheaves subject to two
1825	geometric conditions: a coherent quotient must have \emph{schematic support
1826	proper over the base}, and the Grassmannian's quotients must be \emph{locally
1827	free of a fixed rank}. This section introduces both predicates.
1828	
1829	\begin{definition}
1830	
1831	\leanok
1832	[Annihilator ideal sheaf of a sheaf of modules]
1833	  \label{def:modules_annihilator}
1834	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator}
1835	  \uses{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1836	  % NOTE: The definition itself is formalized and axiom-clean; the inclusion direction
1837	  % \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U) \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\)
1838	  % (\cref{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}) is closed. The reverse inclusion, which identifies
1839	  % the annihilator sheaf with the module-annihilator on affine opens, remains bridge-gated on
1840	  % \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}.
1841	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
1842	  % Polynomials" (read from
1843	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
1844	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1845	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
1846	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
1847	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
1848	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$."
1849	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
1850	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of
1851	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules that is \emph{quasi-coherent and of finite type}
1852	  (over a locally noetherian base, so that on each affine open \(U\) the section
1853	  module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is a finitely generated \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module).
1854	  The \emph{annihilator ideal sheaf} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the ideal sheaf
1855	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) defined on affine opens: for each affine open
1856	  \(U \subseteq X\),
1857	  \[
1858	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
1859	    \;=\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr)
1860	    \;\subseteq\; \mathcal{O}_X(U),
1861	  \]
1862	  the annihilator of the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\). These
1863	  local data patch to a coherent ideal sheaf on \(X\) whose vanishing locus is
1864	  the set-theoretic support of \(\mathcal{F}\).
1865	
1866	  \emph{Well-definedness on basic opens.} The coherence condition for an ideal
1867	  sheaf requires that, for an affine open \(U\) and \(f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)\),
1868	  the section \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)\) restricts correctly to the basic
1869	  open \(D(f) \subseteq U\), i.e. that
1870	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(D(f))}(\mathcal{F}(D(f)))\) is the extension of
1871	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\) along the localization map
1872	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \to \mathcal{O}_X(D(f))\). This is exactly where the two
1873	  hypotheses enter. The bridge
1874	  \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen} supplies, for a quasi-coherent
1875	  finite-type \(\mathcal{F}\), that \(\mathcal{O}_X(D(f)) = (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\)
1876	  and that the restriction \(\mathcal{F}(U) \to \mathcal{F}(D(f))\) exhibits
1877	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(f))\) as the localized module \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
1878	  \mathcal{F}(U)\); the algebra engine
1879	  \cref{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map} then yields, using that
1880	  \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is finitely generated, the required identity
1881	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{(\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f}\bigl((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
1882	  \mathcal{F}(U)\bigr) = \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U)) \cdot
1883	  (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\). Thus the local annihilators are compatible with basic-open
1884	  restriction, which is the patching datum that makes \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\)
1885	  a well-defined ideal sheaf. The finite-type hypothesis is genuinely required:
1886	  without finite generation of \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) the annihilator need not commute
1887	  with localization, and the local data fail to patch.
1888	
1889	  \emph{Note.} Mathlib does not carry an annihilator for sheaves of modules on a scheme.
1890	\end{definition}
1891	
1892	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1893	[Annihilator ideal bounded by the module annihilator on affine opens]
1894	  \label{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}
1895	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator_ideal_le}
1896	  \uses{def:modules_annihilator}

1898	  \(U \subseteq X\) an affine open. Then the section over \(U\) of the annihilator
1899	  ideal sheaf \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}) is
1900	  contained in the module-annihilator of the sections of \(\mathcal{F}\) over
1901	  \(U\):
1902	  \[
1903	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
1904	    \;\subseteq\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr).
1905	  \]
1906	  This is the inclusion direction available directly from the definition of
1907	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\); the reverse inclusion is the basic-open coherence
1908	  statement gated on \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}. Proved directly
1909	  in Lean. It supports the well-definedness of the schematic support
1910	  (\cref{def:schematic_support}).
1911	\end{lemma}

1913	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1914	[Annihilator of a finitely generated module commutes with
1915	  localization]
1916	  \label{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map}
1917	  \lean{Module.annihilator_isLocalizedModule_eq_map}
1918	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(S \subseteq R\) be a multiplicative
Let me continue reading the blueprint chapter to get the remaining sections.
Let me read the rest of the blueprint chapter.

1919	  submonoid, and let \(R_S = S^{-1}R\) be the localization of \(R\) at \(S\). Let
1920	  \(M\) be a \emph{finitely generated} \(R\)-module and let \(f : M \to M_S\) be
1921	  an \(R\)-linear map exhibiting \(M_S\) as the localized module \(S^{-1}M\)
1922	  (i.e.\ \(f\) is a localization of \(M\) at \(S\)). Then the annihilator of
1923	  \(M_S\) over \(R_S\) is the extension of the annihilator of \(M\) along the
1924	  structure map \(R \to R_S\):
1925	  \[
1926	    \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)
1927	    \;=\; (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S
1928	    \;=\; \mathrm{map}\bigl(R \to R_S\bigr)\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}_R M\bigr).
1929	  \]
1930	  Equivalently, for finitely generated \(M\) the annihilator commutes with
1931	  localization: \(\mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M) = S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M)\).
1932	
1933	  \emph{The finite-generation hypothesis is essential.} For a module that is not
1934	  finitely generated the inclusion \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M) \subseteq
1935	  \mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M)\) can be strict, so the identity fails. This is the
1936	  standard commutative-algebra fact that the annihilator of a finite module
1937	  localizes; it is recorded here as the load-bearing algebra engine for the
1938	  basic-open coherence of \cref{def:modules_annihilator}.
1939	\end{lemma}
1940	
1941	\begin{proof}
1942	  \(\supseteq\): if \(r \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\), then \(r\) kills every element of
1943	  \(M\), hence its image \(r/1 \in R_S\) kills every element of \(M_S\) (each such
1944	  element is \(m/s = (1/s)\cdot f(m)\) for some \(m \in M\), \(s \in S\), and
1945	  \((r/1)\cdot(m/s) = (1/s)\cdot f(r\,m) = 0\)). Thus
1946	  \((\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\).
1947	
1948	  \(\subseteq\): write \(M = \langle m_1, \dots, m_n\rangle\) using the
1949	  finite-generation hypothesis, and let \(y \in \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\). Write
1950	  \(y = a/s\) with \(a \in R\), \(s \in S\). For each generator \(m_i\) the element
1951	  \(y\cdot f(m_i) = (a/s)\cdot f(m_i) = 0\) in \(M_S\); by the definition of the
1952	  localized module this means there is \(u_i \in S\) with \(u_i\,a\,m_i = 0\) in
1953	  \(M\). Put \(u = \prod_{i=1}^n u_i \in S\). Then \(u\,a\) kills every generator
1954	  \(m_i\), hence kills all of \(M\), so \(u\,a \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\). Finally
1955	  \(y = a/s = (u\,a)/(u\,s)\) lies in \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}_R M =
1956	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\). Therefore \(\mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S) \subseteq
1957	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\), and combined with the reverse inclusion the two
1958	  ideals coincide. The use of a single common multiplier \(u = \prod_i u_i\) is
1959	  exactly the step that requires finitely many generators.
1960	\end{proof}

1962	\begin{lemma}[Sections on a basic open localize the affine ring]
1963	  \label{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
1964	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAffineOpen.isLocalization_basicOpen}
1965	  \mathlibok
1966	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
1967	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}).}
1968	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(U \subseteq X\) an affine open, and
1969	  \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then the restriction map
1970	  \(\Gamma(X, U) \to \Gamma\bigl(X,\, D(f)\bigr)\) on the basic open
1971	  \(D(f) = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,f\) exhibits \(\Gamma(X, D(f))\) as the
1972	  localization of \(\Gamma(X, U)\) away from \(f\); that is, it is
1973	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalization.Away}\,f\), so
1974	  \(\Gamma(X, D(f)) = (\Gamma(X, U))_f\) as a \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-algebra.
1975	\end{lemma}

1977	\begin{lemma}[Quasi-coherent sections on a basic open are the localized module]
1978	  \label{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1979	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isLocalizedModule_basicOpen}
1980	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
1981	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Schemes" (chapter tag 020J), \S 7
1982	  % "Quasi-coherent sheaves on affines", Lemma \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves},
1983	  % item (4) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L691-L702).
1984	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1985	  % "Let $R$ be a ring. Let $M$ be an $R$-module. Let $\widetilde M$
1986	  %  be the sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$-modules
1987	  %  associated to $M$.
1988	  %  [...]
1989	  %  \item For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
1990	  %  as an $R_f$-module."
1991	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Schemes", \S 7, Lemma on the sheaves
1992	  \(\widetilde M\) on \(\Spec(R)\), item (4).}
1993	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a \emph{quasi-coherent} sheaf
1994	  of modules on \(X\). Let \(U \subseteq X\) be an
1995	  affine open and \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then:
1996	  \begin{enumerate}
1997	    \item \(\Gamma\bigl(X, D(f)\bigr)\) is the localization \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)
1998	      of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\)
1999	      (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}); and
2000	    \item the section-restriction map
2001	      \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}\bigl(D(f)\bigr)\) exhibits
2002	      \(\mathcal{M}(D(f))\) as the localized module
2003	      \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}\,\mathcal{M}(U)\); that is, it is a localization
2004	      of the \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(\mathcal{M}(U)\) at the submonoid
2005	      \(\mathrm{powers}\,f = \{1, f, f^2, \dots\}\).
2006	  \end{enumerate}
2007	\end{lemma}
2008	
2009	\begin{proof}
2010	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
2011	  Part (1) is \cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}, the Mathlib statement
2012	  that on an affine open \(U\) the sections over the basic open \(D(f)\) form the
2013	  localization of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\).
2014	
2015	  For part (2), identify the affine open \(U\) with \(\Spec \Gamma(X, U)\). Over
2016	  this affine, a quasi-coherent sheaf is \(\widetilde{N}\) for the
2017	  \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\), the sheaf associated to
2018	  \(N\) (no finiteness of \(N\) is required for this step). The Spec-local
2019	  computation of the sections of \(\widetilde{N}\) on a
2020	  basic open gives \(\Gamma\bigl(D(f), \widetilde{N}\bigr) = N_f\) as an
2021	  \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)-module, and the restriction map
2022	  \(\Gamma(\Spec \Gamma(X,U), \widetilde{N}) = N \to N_f\) is precisely the
2023	  localization homomorphism \(N \to (\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1} N\). Transporting
2024	  this identification across the affine isomorphism
2025	  \(U \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, U)\) -- under which \(\mathcal{M}|_U\) corresponds to
2026	  \(\widetilde{N}\) by quasi-coherence and the basic open \(D(f) \subseteq U\)
2027	  corresponds to the standard open \(D(f) \subseteq \Spec\Gamma(X,U)\) -- shows
2028	  that the restriction \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}(D(f))\) is the
2029	  localization of \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\), i.e.\ it is a
2030	  localized module at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\). The substantive content is this
2031	  transport of the Spec-local localization fact for the sheaf \(\widetilde{N}\)
2032	  to a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{M}\) via the affine identification.
2033	\end{proof}

2082	\begin{lemma}[Properness as a morphism property]
2083	  \label{lem:isProper_mathlib}
2084	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsProper}
2085	  \mathlibok
2086	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
2087	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Proper}).}
2088	  A morphism \(f : X \to S\) of schemes is \emph{proper} iff it is separated,
2089	  universally closed, and locally of finite type. Properness is stable under base
2090	  change: if \(f : X \to S\) is proper and \(g : T \to S\) is any morphism then
2091	  the base change \(X \times_S T \to T\) is proper. Consequently the base-change
2092	  clause that Nitsure invokes (``properness \ldots\ preserved by base-change'')
2093	  applies directly to the proper-support condition below.
2094	\end{lemma}

2202	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
2203	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
2204	  \(L\) a line bundle on \(X\), \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\), and
2205	  \(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). The \emph{Quot functor with Hilbert
2206	  polynomial \(\Phi\)}
2207	  \[
2208	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set}
2209	  \]
2210	  sends an \(S\)-scheme \(T \to S\) to the set of equivalence classes
2211	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) of pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) where
2212	  \begin{enumerate}
2213	    \item \(\mathcal{F}\) is a coherent sheaf on \(X_T = X \times_S T\) whose
2214	      schematic support is proper over \(T\) (\cref{def:has_proper_support}) and
2215	      which is flat over \(T\);
2216	    \item \(q : E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) is a surjective
2217	      \(\mathcal{O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism, where \(E_T\) is the pull-back
2218	      of \(E\) along \(X_T \to X\);
2219	    \item for every \(t \in T\) the Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}\)
2220	      with respect to \(L|_{X_t}\) (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}) equals
2221	      \(\Phi\).
2222	  \end{enumerate}
2223	  Two pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) and \((\mathcal{F}', q')\) are equivalent iff
2224	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). On morphisms \(f : T' \to T\) over \(S\), the functor
2225	  acts by pulling back the quotient along \(X_{T'} \to X_T\) (well-defined
2226	  since tensor product is right-exact and preserves both flatness and
2227	  properness). The Hilbert scheme is the special case
2228	  \(\Hilb^{\Phi,L}_{X/S} := \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\).
2229	\end{definition}

2235	locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian over \(S\). Since Mathlib carries

2237	linear algebra), we package the construction here.

2276	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(V\) be a locally free
2277	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\). For an integer \(1 \le d \le r\), the

2288	  \(\mathrm{Grass}(V, d) = \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_S}_{V/S/S}\): the Quot functor

2376	% [Nitsure], \S 1 (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,

2378	% ("Construction by gluing together affine patches", "Separatedness",

2381	% SOURCE QUOTE comment of the theorem statement above.
2382	\begin{proof}
2383	  
2384	  Over \(S = \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) and \(V = \mathcal{O}_S^{\oplus r}\) the
2385	  Grassmannian \(\mathrm{Gr}(r, d)\) is constructed by gluing
2386	  \(\binom{r}{d}\) affine charts \(U^I = \Spec\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) indexed by
2387	  size-\(d\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\). The chart \(U^I\)
2388	  carries the matrix \(X^I\) of size \(d \times r\) whose \(I\)-th \(d \times d\)
2389	  minor is the identity, with the remaining \(d(r-d)\) entries free
2390	  variables (so \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\)). The
2391	  transition maps \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I \cap U^J \to U^J \cap U^I\) defined
2392	  by \(X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) on the locus where \(\det X^I_J\) is
2393	  invertible satisfy the cocycle condition
2394	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\). The glued
2395	  \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec\mathbb{Z}\) is smooth of relative dimension
2396	  \(d(r-d)\). Separatedness is checked from the diagonal
2397	  \(\Delta_{I,J} \subset U^I \times U^J\) cut out by \(X^J_I X^I - X^J = 0\).
2398	  Properness follows from the valuative criterion for DVRs: given
2399	  \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over a DVR \(R\) with fraction
2400	  field \(K\), factoring through some chart \(U^I\) via a ring map
2401	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\), choose \(J\) minimising the valuation
2402	  \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) of the minor; on the chart \(U^J\) all entries have
2403	  non-negative valuation, so \(f\) extends uniquely to \(\Spec R\).
2404	
2405	  The universal quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\oplus r}
2406	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) is given on \(U^I\) by the matrix \(X^I\)
2407	  and glued by \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I \cap U^J)\). The
2408	  determinant line bundle \(\det\mathcal{U}\) has transition functions
2409	  \(\det g_{I,J} = 1/P^I_J\); the global sections
2410	  \(\sigma_I \in \Gamma(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), \det\mathcal{U})\) defined by
2411	  \(\sigma_I|_{U^J} = P^J_I\) form a base-point-free linear system separating
2412	  points over \(\Spec\mathbb{Z}\), giving an embedding into
2413	  \(\mathbb{P}^{\binom{r}{d}-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\), which is closed by
2414	  properness. Representability of \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d)\) is then verified
2415	  on the universal pair \((\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), u)\): a quotient
2416	  \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\oplus r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of
2417	  rank \(d\) on \(T\) is everywhere locally trivialised; on the locus where
2418	  the columns indexed by \(I\) are a basis the matrix of \(q\) has the form
2419	  \(X^I\), defining the classifying morphism \(T \to U^I \subset
2420	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Base change from \(\mathbb{Z}\) to a general noetherian
2421	  base \(S\) with a vector bundle \(V\) is direct: trivialise \(V\) on an open
2422	  cover and glue. For non-trivial \(V\) the same construction produces
2423	  \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d)\) with Pl\"ucker embedding into
2424	  \(\mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^d V)\).
2425	\end{proof}

## Severity summary

### must-fix-this-iter

**Finding 1 — Signature mismatch: `Grassmannian.representable`.**

The Lean statement (line 225):
```lean
theorem Grassmannian.representable {S : Scheme.{u}} [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
    (V : S.Modules) (d : ℕ) :
    ∃ (Y : Over S), Nonempty ((Grassmannian V d).RepresentableBy Y) := by sorry
```
The blueprint's prose requires: "representable by a **smooth projective** S-scheme Gr_S(V,d) → S of **relative dimension d(r-d)**, equipped with a **tautological rank-d quotient** π*V ↠ U. The determinant line bundle det(U) is **relatively very ample**, giving a **Plucker closed embedding** Gr_S(V,d) ↪ ℙ_S(⋀^d V)." Additionally, V should be **locally free of rank r** and **1 ≤ d ≤ r** — neither hypothesis appears.

The blueprint's own NOTE confirms: "The Lean statement … is currently a weakened existence skeleton that omits smoothness, properness, relative dimension d(r-d), the tautological rank-d quotient, and the Plucker embedding. The `\lean{}` pin above points at a declaration that under-delivers the prose statement; it should be strengthened or split into a separate skeleton label."

This is a direct blueprint-acknowledged signature mismatch. Per the severity rules, this is must-fix-this-iter.

`AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing`, `AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule`, `AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_fg`, and `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomialOfSectionModule` are all `\lean{}`-pinned in this chapter (`% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean`) but absent from the Lean file. Nine declarations are `private` in Lean but referenced by `\lean{}` pins. This prevents `sync_leanok` from verifying the `\leanok` markers on those blocks. The exposure of private `IsRatHilb` in the public theorem's return type is an API inconsistency. Blueprint acknowledges and authorizes deferral.
